modules.moduleDescription: This module helps students brainstorm ideas that fully respond to the prompt, understand the writer’s purpose regarding the target audience, and flesh out complex or abstract concepts that need further context or content.
modules.name: Planning

prompts.name: Topic Brainstorming
prompts.prompt: You are a kind, helpful tutor with an understanding of the applicable subject matter area and good research and writing skills. You want to help an undergraduate student brainstorm ideas for a topic and provide useful, specific advice. Use language and speak in a way that is appropriate for the student's college level. Ask the following 3 questions one at a time. Too many questions are overwhelming. Always wait for the student to respond before moving on. In your feedback always use the information you gained from those questions. Start by asking the student what subject the writing is for. Then ask for any relevant details of the assignment. Then ask what they might be interested in writing about. Once they have responded, help them come up with a specific topic for the assignment by giving them a list of 5 topic ideas that align with the subject and the student’s areas of interest. Offer to generate more ideas or expand on ideas with the student. At the end of the conversation, let the student know they can always come back to the conversation and they might consider trying the "Topic Researching" prompt if you're ready to move to that or "Topic Background Knowledge" prompt if you want some general information on your topic next.

prompts.name: Topic Researching
prompts.prompt: Act as a research librarian at a large research institution who is very knowledgeable in conducting efficient, effective database searches. Start by asking the student their topic or research question. Then ask the student if they want help with identifying library databases that would be good to search for this topic, or if they want help with identifying keywords they can use when searching library databases. If the student says they want help with identifying library databases, recommend 2 or 3 library databases that would be good to search for this topic then ask if they want help on key words. If they want help with identifying keywords, please list some keywords they can use when searching library databases. Suggest a Boolean search that might be useful to optimize their research query. At the end of the conversation, let the student know they can always come back to the conversation and they might consider trying either (1) the "Topic Brainstorming" prompt if you want to brainstorm further or (2) "Topic Background Knowledge" prompt if you want some general information on your topic next.

prompts.name: Thesis Creation
prompts.prompt: You are a friendly and helpful writing tutor who assists undergraduate students in the planning process for writing assignments. Your goal is to guide students through selecting a topic, developing a thesis, and creating an outline by asking them questions and encouraging them to think deeply about their ideas. You want to help them by asking questions, rather than doing the work for them. Remember that good tutors can help someone learn by assessing prior knowledge, giving them adaptive explanations, providing examples, and asking open ended questions that help them construct their own knowledge. Tutors should guide students and give hints and ask leading questions. Tutors should also assess student knowledge by asking them to explain something in their own words, give an example, or apply their knowledge. Do not generate the student side of the conversation. Keep your questions or explanations short and simple, making them accessible to middle schoolers. Discuss each question through a dialogue of up to three exchanges to prevent the student from becoming fatigued or distracted. Follow the steps below. Proceed step-by-step, asking one question at a time. Do not deviate from the step-by-step instructions and guide each step as an effective tutor, briefly explaining why it's important. When a student is unsure or not responding, first guide them with thought-provoking questions, then offer multiple examples to spark ideas, encouraging them to choose their own path. Here are the steps: (1) First introduce yourself to the student without mentioning your name and tell the student you are there to help plan their writing. Then ask the student the following questions about the assignment, only one at a time. Ask about the genre (argumentative, informative, narrative, report, etc.). Wait for the response. Then ask about the prompt or special instructions from the teacher, if they have any. Wait for the response. (2) Then ask about the topic. Help them refine and expand their own ideas based on their interests and the assignment requirements. Ask them why they find this topic interesting and which specific aspect they want to discuss in their writing. If their topic is not specific enough, help them narrow it down or improve it by asking questions. If they are not sure about their topic, recommend potential topics and encourage them to decide on their own. (3) Once a topic is chosen, guide the student in developing a thesis statement by asking a series of questions, one at a time. Ask them what they want to highlight about their topic and why it's important. Encourage them to think about their viewpoint and how they can support it with evidence. Help them articulate their thoughts by asking guiding questions. Once, the student develops the thesis, provide feedback and help refine it, ensuring it aligns with their chosen topic and argument. Do not write or revise the thesis on behalf of the student, but provide revision examples using a different topic if necessary. Do not make specific suggestions or examples for wording or sentence. Do not write on behalf of the student, even if asked. Wrap up by complimenting their efforts and explaining that this can serve as a starting point and they can keep improving it as they move forward. Now, start by asking the first question and proceed step-by-step, asking one question at a time.

prompts.name: Topic Background Knowledge
prompts.prompt: You are a kind, helpful tutor with an understanding of the applicable subject matter area and good research and writing skills. You want to help an undergraduate student get background knowledge on a specific topic and provide useful, specific advice. Use language and speak in a way that is appropriate for the student's college level. Ask the following 3 questions one at a time. Too many questions are overwhelming. Always wait for the student to respond before moving on. In your feedback always use the information you gained from those questions. Start by asking the student what specific topic they are looking for background knowledge on. Then ask if there is anything specific they are looking to understand about that specific topic, such as a disciplinary angle on the topic or a certain area of interest within the broader topic. Based on their response, provide the user with background knowledge on their specific topic, customized according to what they shared about their major or a specific area of interest within that topic. Then offer to expand on specific subtopics or parts of your response. At the end of the conversation, let the student know they can always come back to the conversation and they might consider trying the "Topic Researching" prompt if you want help with researching the topic further or "Topic Brainstorming" prompt if you want to further brainstorm topics.

prompts.name: Text Summaries
prompts.prompt: You are now a tool called “Content Summarizer”. Your purpose is to analyze content provided by the user and create a summary of that content that can be easily reviewed by the user to pull out the main ideas and key takeaways. You should assume that the user is someone researching the topic of the content that is provided, so it is important to provide valuable insights. Here is how you will fulfill your purpose:  Step 1. Analyze the content the user provides you. This may take the form of some text that is copied and pasted in, or it could be an attachment such as a PDF, a Word document, or an image of some content. The user may also provide a URL to a webpage. If you have access, review the content of the page. If you do not, let the user know and suggest that they provide the content to you through other means.  Regardless of what content is provided to you, your analysis should be thorough and of the caliber one would expect from an academic researcher.  Step 2. Once you have analyzed the content, provide your summary as follows:  - Give a title for the summary (formatted as H3)  - Provide a heading titled “Overview” (formatted as H4) followed by a one-paragraph summary that explains what the content is about, the types of details it contains, analyses it goes into, and conclusions it draws. This should be 4-5 sentences in length. Be as thorough as you can while fitting within these parameters.  The goal of creating this section is to give the user an understanding of the content so they can determine if it is worth reading in more depth.  - Provide a heading titled “Key Takeaways” (formatted as H4) followed by a bulleted list of two sentence takeaways from the content that was provided. These takeaways should be meaningfully connected to the content that was provided.  Make sure to choose takeaways that would be meaningful for someone researching this topic.  If you understand all the instructions I have given you, please respond as follows: “Please provide the content you would like me to summarize.”

prompts.name: Assignment Checklist
prompts.prompt: You are a helpful and encouraging writing coach who wants to help the student figure out what is required by an assignment. Do the following 3 things in order, one at a time. Always wait for the student to respond before moving on. In your feedback always use the information you gained from those questions. First, start by asking the student to give you the assignment. Second, create a checklist using checkboxes so that they can make sure they address all the requirements of the assignment. Third, ask the student if they have any questions about the checklist. At the end of the conversation, encourage the student to look over the assignment to see if the generated checklist is accurate.

prompts.name: Blank Prompt
prompts.prompt: Begin with a simple greeting and ask the user to type in what they'd like help with today.

messages.content: No problem! If you want to explore more aspects of this topic later, feel free to return. You might also consider using the "Topic Researching" prompt for more research help or the "Topic Brainstorming" prompt to brainstorm related topics.
Is there anything specific you want to understand about securing cyberspace, such as a particular angle or area of interest within the topic?

Here are some basic challenges in securing cyberspace:

1. **Cyber Attacks**: Hackers use malware, phishing, and other tactics to steal data or disrupt systems.

2. **Data Breaches**: Sensitive information can be exposed due to weak security practices.

3. **Advanced Persistent Threats**: These are long-term, targeted attacks often sponsored by criminal groups or nations.

4. **Rapid Technology Change**: New technologies can create security gaps before protections are established.

5. **Human Error**: Mistakes by users, like weak passwords, often lead to security issues.

Would you like more detailed information about any of these challenges?
can you tell me what we are currently doing to deal with these attacks?
DIFRF

Yes, DFIR stands for Digital Forensics and Incident Response. It involves:

1. **Digital Forensics**: The process of collecting and analyzing digital data to investigate security incidents. This includes examining computers, networks, and storage devices for evidence.

2. **Incident Response**: A structured approach to handling and managing security breaches or attacks. This includes identifying, containing, and eradicating the threat, as well as recovering systems.

Would you like more information on a specific part of DFIR?
What specific topic are you looking for background knowledge on?

Certainly! Here are some common motivations behind cyberattacks:

1. **Financial Gain**: Many hackers aim to steal money directly or through data sales.

2. **Espionage**: Some attacks are meant to gather sensitive information for competitive or political advantage.

3. **Disruption**: Hackers may disrupt services to cause chaos, often for political or ideological reasons.

4. **Revenge**: Some attacks are driven by personal grievances or vendettas.

5. **Notoriety**: Gaining fame within hacker communities can also be a motive.

Would you like to explore more about any of these motivations?

Specifically, vulnerability to social engineering

The summary's format (visual appearance) should consist of double-spaced and left-justified text using appropriate font size and style. The form (organization) of the summary should include a brief introduction to the topic to provide context, a summary of the main points/critical details of the article, and a brief conclusion to bring closure to the summary as outlined below (do not use the words Introduction, Body or Conclusion as headings in your summary). Headers are not required for this assignment. The style (tone and formality of the writing) should reflect a formal, scientific tone, avoiding the use of contractions, colloquial expressions, and unnecessary use of first-person references (I, me, my, we, ours, us) and be written to a generalist (non-technical) audience.

Title—State the article's title at the top of the page (Do not use "Journal Article Summary").
Introduction—Begin the summary with an introductory statement (approximately one or two sentences) describing the subject and scope of the article to provide context for the reader.
Body—Summarize the main points presented in the article. Create a brief outline that identifies the significant points discussed in the article. Use your outline as a guide for writing your summary. After each significant point is added to your summary, write a transition statement to smoothly move from one central point to the next. Add appropriate detail to each significant point to provide sufficient clarity for the reader. Omit, find a synonym, or define concepts, terminology, or units of measure appropriate for a generalist (non-technical) reader.
Conclusion—Close your summary by providing a forward-looking statement regarding ongoing research efforts, possible future applications, or the technology's evolutionary path.
Evaluation—In a separate paragraph, assess how well the article conveyed the information to its target audience using clear, concise, and well-organized discourse.
Citing Source Material
No in-text citations are required for this writing assignment. However, the full bibliographic citation of the article you are summarizing should be placed at the end of your summary after the evaluation paragraph. The 3-page limit is for the summary itself. Using a fourth page for the bibliographic citation (or any other citations you wish to include) is acceptable. If you use additional sources to help define any concepts or terminology in the article, you must cite those sources using the IEEE citation styleLinks to an external site.. The Library Research Guide can help you find relevant scientific articles (the electronic link is provided in the References module on the Canvas course space).

AI Detection Process
Turnitin's AI detection scans all major assignments in the course. However, AI detection tools are unreliable. In full transparency, I also use GPTZero. Often, GPTZeroLinks to an external site. is better than the default Turnitin, but as I have said before, AI detection tools are unreliable. ### Exploring Students’ Perceptions of ChatGPT: Thematic Analysis and Follow-Up Survey

#### Overview
The study examines how senior computer engineering students perceive ChatGPT and assesses its potential and challenges in education. It was conducted in two stages: first, students provided written evaluations of ChatGPT after using it in a learning activity. These responses were analyzed to form themes, leading to the creation of a detailed questionnaire. Students admired ChatGPT for its user-friendly interface and helpfulness but noted issues with accuracy and the need for user knowledge. Despite concerns over academic integrity and job impact, students were generally optimistic and interested in using it for learning.

#### Key Takeaways
- Students found ChatGPT interesting, motivating, and helpful for both study and professional use, appreciating its easy-to-use, human-like interface.
- Concerns included ChatGPT's occasional inaccuracies and the requirement for a good background understanding to assess its answers accurately.
- While students recognized ChatGPT's learning benefits, they also highlighted challenges like academic dishonesty and possible job threats.
- Educators are encouraged to guide students on effective use and understanding of ChatGPT and its limitations. Developers should focus on improving accuracy.
- The study highlights ChatGPT's potential in enhancing learning but stresses the importance of understanding its challenges and improving its integration into education.

IEEE EDUCATION SOCIETY SECTION  Received 16 March 2023, accepted 16 April 2023, date of publication 19 April 2023, date of current version 24 April 2023.  Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2023.3268224  Exploring Students’ Perceptions of ChatGPT: Thematic Analysis and Follow-Up Survey  ABDULHADI SHOUFAN  Center for Cyber-Physical Systems, Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, Khalifa University, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates  e-mail: abdulhadi.shoufan@ku.ac.ae This work was supported by the Mohammed Bin Rashid Smart Learning Program, Ministry of Education, United Arab Emirates.  ABSTRACT   ChatGPT has sparked both excitement and skepticism in education. To analyze its impact on teaching and learning it is crucial to understand how students perceive ChatGPT and assess its potential and challenges. Toward this, we conducted a two-stage study with senior students in a computer engineering program ( n   =   56). In the first stage, we asked the students to evaluate ChatGPT using their own words after they used it to complete one learning activity. The returned responses (3136 words) were analyzed by coding and theme building (36 codes and 15 themes). In the second stage, we used the derived codes and themes to create a 27-item questionnaire. The students responded to this questionnaire three weeks later after completing other activities with the help of ChatGPT. The results show that the students admire the capabilities of ChatGPT and find it interesting, motivating, and helpful for study and work. They find it easy to use and appreciate its human-like interface that provides well-structured responses and good explanations. However, many students feel that ChatGPT’s answers are not always accurate and most of them believe that it requires good background knowledge to work with since it does not replace human intelligence. So, most students think that ChatGPT needs to be improved but are optimistic that this will happen soon. When it comes to the negative impact of ChatGPT on learning, academic integrity, jobs, and life, the students are divided. We conclude that ChatGPT can and should be used for learning. However, students should be aware of its limitations. Educators should try using ChatGPT and guide students on effective prompting techniques and how to assess generated responses. The developers should improve their models to enhance the accuracy of given answers. The study provides insights into the capabilities and limitations of ChatGPT in education and informs future research and development.  INDEX TERMS   ChatGPT, students’ perceptions, education.  I. INTRODUCTION  A chatbot is a computer program that simulates a conversa- tion with users through natural language or text, giving the illusion of communicating with a human [1], [2]. Early chat- bots relied on simpler pattern matching and string processing, but more advanced ones now use complex knowledge-based models [3]. Chatbots have long found their way to formal and informal education [4], [5]. They have been used to sup- port learning [6], [7], increase students’ engagement [8], [9], assess students [10], [11], and perform various administrative functions [12], [13].  The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was James Harland.  Educational chatbots, however, face various limitations and challenges [14], [15]. These include difficulties in deal- ing with misspellings, understanding colloquial language, processing student inputs, and mimicking natural conver- sation flow, resulting in a transactional experience devoid of human emotion [9]. Moreover, some researchers high- light the lack of sufficient datasets as a common challenge in educational chatbots leading to learning difficulties and frustration [16]. Chatbots tend to lose their novelty effect over time, as reported in [17]. The authors also highlighted the challenge of comparing results across studies due to the lack of a standard construction protocol for chatbots [17]. As noted in [18], the sustainable use of chatbots for learn- ing depends on their ability to provide smooth access to  VOLUME 11, 2023 This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/   38805 A. Shoufan: Exploring Students’ Perceptions of ChatGPT: Thematic Analysis and Follow-Up Survey  knowledge through efficient storage and retrieval techniques, i.e., knowledge application. To ensure sustainable use, ser- vice providers should prioritize the design of chatbots with features that provide reliable information and the ability to learn and browse in ‘‘anytime and anywhere’’ settings [18]. The emergence of chatbots that utilize large language mod- els (LLM) represents a breakthrough in AI-powered human- computer interaction for knowledge creation. ChatGPT is such chatbot that can generate advanced text and engage in convincing conversations with users. It can help perform various tasks such as writing essays, brainstorming research ideas, conducting literature reviews, enhancing papers, and writing computer code [19]. The abilities of ChatGPT are expected to rapidly expand as it continues to receive new data through user interactions [20]. In education, ChatGPT was received both with admiration and controversy. Some authors believe that AI-based applica- tions such as ChatGPT will inevitably become an integral part of writing, much like calculators and computers have become commonplace in math and science [21]. Therefore, some rec- ommend involving students and instructors with such tools to facilitate teaching and learning, rather than prohibition [22]. In their position paper [23], the authors highlight the oppor- tunities and challenges of ChatGPT for learning and teaching at all levels of education. Accordingly, ChatGPT can help students develop different skills including reading, writing, information analysis, critical thinking, problem-solving, gen- erating practice problems, and research. It supports group and distance learning and empowers learners with disabili- ties [23]. Teachers can use ChatGPT for lesson planning, stu- dent assessment, and professional development. On the other hand, the authors highlight multiple key challenges includ- ing copyright issues, bias, fairness, excessive reliance on ChatGPT by students and teachers, lack of expertise in inte- grating this technology in teaching, the difficulty of distin- guishing model-generated from student-generated answers, cost of training and maintenance, data privacy and security, and sustainable usage [23]. Furthermore, ChatGPT operates differently from search engines like Google as it doesn’t scan the internet for up-to-date information, and its knowledge is limited to what it acquired before September 2021. Therefore, its inconsistent factual precision has been acknowledged as a notable drawback according to [24]. Academic integrity is probably the most discussed chal- lenge ChatGPT will pose to education [23]. A few stud- ies provide insights into ChatGPT’s abilities to answer test questions. In a preprint, [25] assessed ChatGPT’s ability to generate human-like responses to non-trivial university-level questions in various disciplines. To test this, ChatGPT was asked to create challenging critical thinking questions related to education, machine learning, history, and marketing that targeted undergraduate students. After generating the ques- tions, ChatGPT was asked to provide answers and critically evaluate them. The author evaluated ChatGPT’s responses based on their accuracy, relevance, clarity, precision, depth, breadth, logic, persuasiveness, and originality. He found out that ChatGPT displayed a high level of critical thinking rather than simply retrieving information. The responses generated by ChatGPT were clear, precise, relevant, logically coher- ent, and had sufficient depth and breadth. The author con- cluded that ChatGPT is a   potential threat to the integrity of online exams, particularly in tertiary education settings where such exams are becoming more prevalent , [25]. In [26], the authors evaluated ChatGPT’s performance on four sets of multiple-choice questions from the United States Med- ical Licensing Examination (USMLE). The authors manu- ally entered the questions into ChatGPT and evaluated the answers according to the selected choice as well as the pro- vided explanation in terms of its logical justification and the presence of information internal and external to the question. ChatGPT achieved 42%, 44%, 57.8%, and 64.4% in the four question sets, respectively. However, the performance decreases for questions with a higher difficulty index. The authors concluded that ChatGPT marks significant progress in large language models on the tasks of medical question answering. By performing more than 60% on one question set, the model achieves the equivalent of a passing score for a third-year medical student. Additionally, ChatGPT could provide logic and informational context across most answers. The authors recommended using ChatGPT as an interactive tool for medical education to support learning [26]. In [27], the authors carried out a similar study and assessed Chat- GPT’s effectiveness in the USMLE exam. They selected three question types: open-ended prompts without answer choices, and multiple-choice questions with and without mandatory justification. The authors discovered that ChatGPT attained scores near or at the passing threshold without specialized training or reinforcement. Moreover, ChatGPT’s explana- tions were coherent and insightful. The authors suggested that ChatGPT could be used in medical education and potentially support clinical decision-making [27]. Although these studies provide initial insights into the potentials and challenges of ChatGPT, they do this from an educator rather than a student perspective. In particular, in all the cited papers, the system was prompted and the responses were evaluated by the researchers rather than by students. To fully understand ChatGPT’s impact on educa- tion, we need to investigate students’ experience with this language model and their perceptions of it. Students’ per- ceptions are highly relevant for education as they can have a significant impact on their motivation, engagement, and academic achievement [28]. When students have positive perceptions of their learning experience, they are more likely to be engaged and motivated to learn, which can lead to better academic outcomes. On the other hand, when students have negative perceptions of their learning experience, they may become disengaged, less motivated, and less likely to achieve academic success [29]. To our knowledge, students’ perceptions of ChatGPT have not yet been addressed in the literature. The presented study  38806   VOLUME 11, 2023 A. Shoufan: Exploring Students’ Perceptions of ChatGPT: Thematic Analysis and Follow-Up Survey  aims to close this gap by addressing the following research questions: 1)   How do students perceive ChatGPT in the context of learning? 2)   What are ChatGPT’s pros and cons from students’ perspectives? To assure that the students express their perceptions based on experience, they were asked to complete some activities using this application before they responded to our surveys. To explore students’ thoughts without restriction, we first asked them to use their own words to express their opin- ions as a response to an open-ended question. Their com- ments were then analyzed thematically to identify relevant strengths and weaknesses of ChatGPT. The outcomes of this thematic analysis were then used to develop a questionnaire to assess these pros and cons quantitatively from students’ perspectives. A framework for using ChatGPT was created that informs educators and researchers about relevant aspects of ChatGPT in education and necessary actions and research in this area. The rest of the paper is structured as follows. Section II outlines the methods used in this study. Section III summa- rizes the results and Section IV discusses them. Section V describes the implications of this research and its limitations and concludes the paper.  II. METHODS  The   methods   employed   in   this   study   are   illustrated in Figure 1, where the blocks on the left side depict the contributions made by the students.  A. PARTICIPANTS  The   participants   of   this   study   are   56   senior   students (66% males) taking a core course on embedded systems in a computer engineering program in Spring 2023.  B. CONTEXT  The course teaches the foundations of embedded systems including microcontroller system architecture, memory opti- mization, hardware/software interfacing, register-level pro- gramming, interrupts, timers, analog signal processing, pulse wide modulation, serial communication, and real-time oper- ating systems. A lecture-free method is used for teaching the course, where the students receive an Ardunio-based kit and complete learning activities using Moodle in the classroom or at home [30].  C. ChatGPT-BASED ACTIVITIES  We created four ungraded quizzes on Moodle that the stu- dents had to complete using ChatGPT. We made sure that the questions relate to topics that were not yet taught in the course so that the students make genuine efforts in prompting ChatGPT to get the answers. The activities included con- ceptual questions, code completion tasks, and code analysis questions. The students prompted ChatGPT to obtain the  FIGURE 1.   Methods used in this study.  answers to these questions, entered these answers into Moo- dle, and checked their correctness. This way, the students could evaluate the accuracy of the ChatGPT. Figure 6 in the appendix shows two examples of these questions. To ensure that students used ChatGPT to answer the questions, they were required to include their conversations with ChatGPT in the text fields of additional essay questions within the same quizzes. Note this study focuses on students’ perceptions of ChatGPT and does not analyze their performance in the activities.  D. OPEN-ENDED QUESTION  After completing the first activity, the students were asked to respond to the following open-ended question through Moodle:  ‘‘What do you think of ChatGPT? Think deeply and write down whatever comes into your mind!’’ . The purpose of this question is to efficiently solicit stu- dents’ opinions without limitations as a basis for generating specific questionnaire items in the second stage of the study.  E. THEMATIC ANALYSIS OF STUDENTS’ RESPONSES  Students’ responses to the open question were cleaned and analyzed using Taguette [31]. This free application supports analyzing qualitative data such as interview transcripts, sur- vey responses, and open-ended survey questions. Taguette enables users to encode different segments of text data, mak- ing it easier to identify patterns or themes. Figure 2 shows a screenshot of Taguette with the processed response file. To encode a text segment, we highlight it and assign a tag. New tags (codes) are added when needed. Taguette allows multiple tagging if a text segment contains different ideas. The left-side tab in Figure 2 shows part of the evolved tags  VOLUME 11, 2023   38807 A. Shoufan: Exploring Students’ Perceptions of ChatGPT: Thematic Analysis and Follow-Up Survey  FIGURE 2.   Taguette-The software used for coding students responses to the open-ended question: ‘‘What do you think of ChatGPT? Think deeply and write down whatever comes into your mind!.’’  during the coding. After completion, the data were exported to Excel for further analysis. Theme building is a mental step that consists in identifying similar codes and grouping them into themes. The result section will provide insights into the evolved codes and created schemes.  F. 27-ITEM QUESTIONNAIRE  The use of an open question allowed the students to express their thoughts without limitations, and the subsequent the- matic analysis aimed to categorize these thoughts into pat- terns (codes and themes). The aim of the questionnaire it returns these patterns to all students so that every student can assess every item which can enable a quantitative assessment of the different aspects of ChatGPT. A 27-item question- naire was developed and posted on Moodle based on the derived codes and themes. Each item required students to indicate their level of agreement with a given statement using a 5-point Likert scale ( Yes very much ,   Yes ,   Average ,   No , and  Not at all ). Since the questionnaire items resulted from the thematic analysis, they will be described in the result section. The students responded to this questionnaire after completing three more ChatGPT activities.  G. ANALYZING STUDENTS’ RESPONSES TO THE QUESTIONNAIRE  Students’ responses to the questionnaire were evaluated using   frequency   analysis.   To   compare   their   relevance, each item was assigned an   average rate   ( AR ) that varies between 1 and 5.   AR   is calculated as :  AR   =   5   ×   f 5   +   4   ×   f 4   +   3   ×   f 3   +   2   ×   f 2   +   1   ×   f 1 ,  where   f 5 ,   f 4 ,   f 3 ,   f 2 , and   f 1   are the relative frequencies of using the rates   Yes very much ,   Yes ,   Average ,   No , and   Not at all , respectively. However, the students tend to think that this platform will pose other threats such including manipulations and malicious use ( AR   =   3 . 44).  IV. DISCUSSION  With the emergence of large language models, a principal question arises for education: Will these models be a chance or a challenge to today’s teaching and learning systems? Students are core players in this context. Understanding their perceptions is indispensable for addressing this question. In this study, the students have provided comprehensive and insightful thoughts about ChatGPT that helped in design- ing questionnaire items. The responses to the questionnaire allowed us to assess the relevance of various aspects of using ChatGPT in education. Figure 5 summarizes the main find- ings of this study as a framework for using ChatGPT in edu- cation. The middle branch represents five core components including basic requirements, students’ interaction with the system, its impact on learning, its long-term impact, and the development of affections. To utilize ChatGPT effectively, students must have an adequate background in the relevant field of study so that they can generate appropriate prompts and critically evaluate the responses provided by the system. This suggests that ChatGPT, at least at the current time, should not be relied on as a sole resource for learning by students who don’t have sufficient prior knowledge. But what about using ChatGPT by students who have this knowledge? Isn’t this a risk to academic integrity? Our students assessed this risk moder- ately. In contrast, some researchers highlight academic dis- honesty as a major challenge of large language models [25]. This suggests that educators and educational institutions must reconsider how to assess students and implement new mea- sures to prevent academic misconduct. In [24], the authors presented some ideas to avoid academic dishonesty in the era of AI-powered language models. Accordingly, we should move away from overly formulaic tests and opt more for assessment methods that cultivate students’ creative and crit- ical thinking abilities. This can be achieved by implementing various strategies such as in-class assessments, presentations, and performances, allowing for student choice in topic selec- tion, and using authentic assessment methods that reflect real- world situations [24]. Interacting with ChatGPT is made easy and engaging through its natural language conversations. Students may feel comfortable and less thoughtful when composing their queries leading to less accurate responses. Furthermore, the good explanations provided by ChatGPT can create a false sense of accuracy. Therefore, it is crucial for educators and instructors to guide students on effective techniques for gen- erating prompts and evaluating responses. Large language models like ChatGPT don’t just retrieve information like traditional search engines but generate new knowledge by inference starting from user prompts. Prompt engineering is an emerging topic in large language models that provides an understanding of how prompting affects the performance of these models [32], [33]. Despite the trickiness of prompting and the modest accu- racy,   the   students   perceive   ChatGPT   as   a   helpful   and efficient tool for learning and professional life. The perceived usefulness   and   ease   of   use   are   determinants   for   the  VOLUME 11, 2023   38813 A. The missing words in the left-side question are   loop ,   malloc ,   fact   ,   j   , and   free   in order. The missing words in right-side question are   byte   and   F   in order.  behavioral intention to use technology according to the tech- nology acceptance model (TAM) [34]. On the other hand, the students evaluate the disadvantages of ChatGPT for learn- ing moderately. So, they don’t see the system as a major threat to learning or academic integrity. This is in line with and probably due to their perception that ChatGPT is not a golden source of knowledge. Rather, using it requires back- ground knowledge and thoughtful engagement in crafting the prompts and evaluating the responses. Similarly, the negative impact of ChatGPT on job opportunities is evaluated moder- ately. Again, this is in line with other perceptions, e.g., that ChatGPT does not replace human intelligence and is good a complementary rather than sufficient resource. The students expressed high levels of interest, admiration, and motivation toward ChatGPT. Interest is highly relevant for learning since it enhances students’ self-regulation, col- laboration, problem-solving, and joy of learning [35]. Many factors may have contributed to these attitudes such as the good explanations, the ease of use, the human-like conver- sation, and the usefulness for learning. Students’ responses to the items related to the positive themes.  how this situational interest can develop into individual inter- est that motivates the long-term usage of this technology [36].  V. IMPLICATIONS, LIMITATIONS, AND CONCLUSION  This   study   has   several   implications   for   education   and research: 1)   Despite its moderate answering accuracy, ChatGPT seems to be an attractive platform for students. They are impressed, interested, motivated, and optimistic about it. Educators should investigate how to make the best out of this interest. They should explore the capabilities and deficits of ChatGPT in their fields and teach students how to use it beneficially. 2)   Research in educational psychology is needed to under- stand what makes ChatGPT so attractive and what can be done to maintain students’ interest in this platform. This study has pointed to some factors that can be con- sidered such as the explanation quality and the human- like interaction. 3)   The study has highlighted some factors that are typ- ically relevant for technology acceptance including the usefulness and ease of use. More research is needed to understand these and other factors using  VOLUME 11, 2023   38815 A. Shoufan: Exploring Students’ Perceptions of ChatGPT: Thematic Analysis and Follow-Up Survey  FIGURE 8.   Students’ responses to the items related to the negative themes.  modern models such as the unified theory of accep- tance and use of technology (UTAUT) [37]. This theory explains the behavioral intention to use tech- nology by four constructs: performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, and facilitating con- ditions. For instance, ChatGPT has enjoyed enormous attention in media and societies. Were students’ per- ceptions affected by this hype? Furthermore, UTAUT considers several moderators such as gender, age, and experience. More research is needed to understand the impact of these moderators. 4)   The role of human intelligence and background knowl- edge is not yet understood. Empirical research is needed to link these factors to the quality of students’ prompts and ChatGPT’s responses. Prompt engineer- ing is an emerging field that addresses such questions not only for training large language models but also for using these models [38]. 5)   ChatGPT shows good performance on certain tasks such as writing essays, and it is expected to improve soon. Educators should get ready for the time when ChatGPT and other large language models will become more capable and accurate and less dependent on prompt quality. This will not only transform the way students acquire knowledge but also disrupt current assessment methods. It is not too early to start thinking about creative assessment techniques for the AI era. This study has several limitations: 1)   The participants of the study are computer engineering students and the activities were related to a specific subject. Although most student responses were general and not specific to their area of study and the course, replicated studies for other educational levels, other programs, and other subjects are needed to confirm the findings of this study. 2)   The thematic analysis used multiple rounds of coding and theme building and multiple refinements based on some expert feedback on a best-effort basis. The study would have benefited from multiple coders and an  38816   VOLUME 11, 2023 A. Shoufan: Exploring Students’ Perceptions of ChatGPT: Thematic Analysis and Follow-Up Survey  inter-rater reliability analysis but, unfortunately, other raters were not available. Note that student responses to the questionnaire support the reliability of the the- matic analysis to some extent. For example, students’ comments with positive attitudes towards ChatGPT in the thematic analysis were confirmed by high rates of the related items of the survey. 3)   The study did not establish correlational links between different codes, themes, or responses to questionnaire items. Creating such relationships require dedicated studies in the future. In conclusion, AI-powered large language models includ- ing ChatGPT will shape a new era of information technology. With the unprecedented interest in ChatGPT and unprece- dented interaction through hundreds of millions of users, we should expect to see a boost in quality soon. Accuracy issues will get less. The better this technology will get, the higher the chances to benefit students’ learning, but the higher the challenge to academic integrity. This revolution in information technology should be best accompanied by revolutionary thoughts about our current methods of teaching and assessment.  A. Certainly! Carbonation is a chemical reaction that occurs in concrete when carbon dioxide (CO₂) from the air is absorbed into the concrete and reacts with minerals, particularly calcium compounds. Here's a simple breakdown:

1. **CO₂ Diffusion**: CO₂ enters the concrete through its pores.
2. **Reaction with Water**: CO₂ dissolves in water present in the concrete, forming carbonic acid.
3. **Calcium Carbonate Formation**: The carbonic acid reacts with calcium hydroxide in the concrete to form calcium carbonate.
4. **Strength Gains and CO₂ Sequestration**: This process helps to lock CO₂ into the concrete, making it part of the solid structure and potentially enhancing strength.

While carbonation helps in reducing atmospheric CO₂, it can also affect the durability of concrete by lowering pH, which may lead to steel reinforcement corrosion. This process varies with concrete composition and environmental conditions.

No, using flue gas for carbonation does not require it to be superheated. Flue gas can be used at the existing temperature and pressure conditions from industrial processes. This makes it a more energy-efficient method compared to supercritical carbonation, as it doesn't need additional energy to heat or pressurize the CO₂.

Instead, the process utilizes the CO₂ in flue gas at its current state to react with the concrete during curing. This approach can still lead to effective CO₂ sequestration and provide benefits in terms of sustainability and cost without the need for extra energy input associated with superheating.

In cement curing, a "hydrated product" refers to the compounds formed when cement reacts with water. This process is called hydration. Here’s how it works:

1. **Hydration Reaction**: When water and cement mix, a chemical reaction occurs, producing new compounds.

2. **Formation of Hydrated Compounds**: These new compounds, mainly calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) and calcium hydroxide, are the "hydrated products."

3. **Structure and Strength**: Calcium silicate hydrate is mainly responsible for the development of strength and the solid structure of hardened concrete.

Hydrated products are crucial for determining the mechanical properties and durability of the concrete as it cures.

This process not only helps in sequestering CO₂ but also can improve certain properties, such as strength and durability, of the cured concrete.

Supercritical carbonation treatment involves using CO₂ in its supercritical state for the carbonation process. Supercritical CO₂ is achieved when CO₂ is heated and pressurized beyond a critical point, where it exhibits properties of both a liquid and a gas.

In this method:

1. **CO₂ State**: The CO₂ becomes supercritical at specific high temperatures and pressures, enhancing its ability to penetrate and react with concrete materials.

2. **Efficiency**: This state allows the CO₂ to diffuse rapidly into the concrete, increasing the carbonation rate and depth more effectively than with gaseous CO₂.

3. **Benefits**: The use of supercritical CO₂ can improve the strength and durability of the concrete while enabling significant CO₂ sequestration.

Though it involves higher costs and complex setups, supercritical carbonation can lead to fast and efficient curing processes due to better CO₂ interaction with the material.

### Summary of CO₂ Sequestration in Concrete

#### Overview
The comprehensive review discusses various studies on CO₂ sequestration through concrete carbonation. Researchers have explored diverse methods to enhance CO₂ uptake in concrete, aiming for environmental sustainability and improved material properties. Studies examine carbonation in concrete slurry waste, recycled aggregates, and different concrete types, identifying methods to increase CO₂ absorption and improve strength. Current research mostly focuses on controlled, accelerated carbonation to boost efficiency. However, there's a gap in understanding carbonation over the long term in real-world conditions. The paper introduces a mathematical model to address this, aiming to predict CO₂ uptake in both ordinary Portland cement (OPC) and geopolymer concretes over time.

#### Key Takeaways
- Studies show concrete can effectively sequester CO₂ through accelerated carbonation, enhancing both sustainability and strength.
- Researchers are developing methods to optimize CO₂ uptake by analyzing concrete materials and processing conditions.
- There is limited knowledge about long-term natural carbonation processes in real-world concrete structures.
- A new model is proposed to quantify CO₂ uptake over a concrete structure's lifetime, improving sustainability assessments in construction.

Gaining early strength in concrete through methods like accelerated carbonation is beneficial for several reasons:

1. **Faster Construction Times**: Early strength allows for quicker formwork removal and reduces waiting times between construction phases, speeding up overall project timelines.

2. **Economic Advantages**: Reduced construction times can lead to cost savings on labor and equipment, which can be significant in large-scale projects.

3. **Improved Scheduling**: Projects with tight deadlines benefit from early strength as they can adhere to stricter timelines and reduce delays.

4. **Quick Use**: Structures or elements that need to be put into service quickly, such as roadways or precast elements, benefit from early strength to meet functional demands sooner.

While the end result in terms of final strength may be similar, achieving early strength provides practical, economic, and operational advantages that can be crucial in many construction scenarios.

### Summary of Concrete Carbonation and CO₂ Sequestration

#### Overview
The paper investigates how concrete can contribute to carbon neutrality despite its significant CO₂ emissions during production. It introduces a model to analyze how CO₂ moves through concrete and turns into carbonates. The research focuses on calculating how much CO₂ concrete can absorb over time, considering different environments. It explores how composition and CO₂ levels impact carbonation depth and CO₂ uptake. The study finds that concrete components like calcium and exposure to high CO₂ levels increase carbonation. Ordinary Portland cement (OPC) and geopolymer (GP) concretes show varying CO₂ absorption rates and depths after years of exposure, providing a basis for designing concrete that captures CO₂ effectively.

#### Key Takeaways
- Concrete production emits 8% of global CO₂, but its chemical makeup allows it to absorb CO₂, leading to potential carbon neutrality.
- Higher contents of calcium and other oxides in concrete, with more CO₂ exposure, enhance concrete's absorption of CO₂, known as carbonation.
- Under natural conditions, OPC concrete absorbs about 5.14% of its weight in CO₂, whereas GP concrete absorbs about 3.38%.
- After 10 years at 50% CO₂ exposure, carbonation depths are around 20 mm for OPC and 25 mm for GP concretes, informing potential design improvements for carbon capture.

2. **Early Strength**: CO₂ curing can rapidly increase early strength, similar to steam curing, due to enhanced reactions facilitated by the increased CO₂ concentration.

Despite this rapid early strength gain, long-term curing and the final setting of concrete will generally follow the typical 28-day timeframe to achieve full structural integrity and performance characteristics.

Yes, the energy required for supercritical carbonation can offset some of the environmental benefits of CO₂ sequestration. Heating and pressurizing CO₂ to reach a supercritical state demands significant energy, which can result in additional CO₂ emissions if the energy sources are not renewable.

This balance is necessary for supercritical carbonation to be a truly sustainable option in reducing the carbon footprint of concrete manufacturing.

With steam curing, concrete often reaches significant strength much faster than with normal curing. Typically:

1. **Initial Strength**: Steam curing can develop substantial early strength within 24 to 48 hours, depending on specific conditions such as temperature, mix design, and cement type.

2. **Full Cure**: While concrete can reach sufficient strength quickly, full cure, achieving its designed strength, usually takes about 28 days overall, similar to standard curing processes.

Steam curing notably accelerates strength gain, allowing for early removal from molds and handling, which is helpful in precast concrete production and projects needing quick turnaround. However, it's essential to note that while strength develops quickly, achieving maximum durability and continued strength gain still follows the typical 28-day curing period.

Belite-rich cement is a type of cement that has a higher proportion of belite, also known as dicalcium silicate (C2S), compared to typical Portland cement. Here's what makes it distinct:

1. **Primary Component**: Belite is one of the major compounds in cement, and it has a slower reaction with water compared to alite (tricalcium silicate, C3S), which is the dominant compound in regular Portland cement.

2. **Slow Strength Gain**: Because belite reacts more slowly, concrete made with belite-rich cement tends to gain strength more gradually. This slower strength development is offset by increased long-term strength.

3. **Benefits**: Belite-rich cement can result in reduced heat of hydration, making it beneficial for large concrete pours where heat management is crucial. It also typically lowers the cement's overall carbon footprint since its production requires less energy and emits less CO₂ than alite-rich cements.

Overall, belite-rich cement offers sustainable advantages but requires consideration of its slower initial strength development in construction planning.

Concrete slurry waste is a byproduct generated during various concrete-related processes. It is a semi-liquid mixture that contains water, cement, aggregate particles, and other materials. Here’s how it is typically produced:

1. **Cutting and Grinding**: Activities like cutting, grinding, or drilling concrete surfaces create slurry from the mix of water and fine concrete particles.
   
2. **Mixing and Cleaning**: It can also come from cleaning concrete trucks or mixers, where leftover concrete mix and washing water form a slurry.

3. **Construction and Demolition**: Generates slurry from excess water mixing with concrete dust and particles during construction or demolition.

Concrete slurry waste poses environmental concerns due to its high pH and potential heavy metal content, making proper disposal and treatment important to prevent pollution and comply with regulations.

Yes, incorporating carbonation during the curing process can be more advantageous for several reasons:

1. **Efficiency**: Accelerated carbonation during curing can speed up the reaction by exposing the wet concrete mix to CO₂, enhancing the absorption rate.

2. **Early Strength**: This method can improve early strength development, allowing concrete to gain significant strength faster than traditional curing.

3. **Environmental Benefits**: By capturing CO₂ during the curing process, this technique helps reduce the carbon footprint of the concrete production.

4. **Energy Use**: It leverages the existing moisture in the curing concrete, potentially reducing the need for additional energy input required in post-cure carbonation processes like gas-solid carbonation.

By integrating carbonation into the curing process, these benefits can be realized more effectively than waiting for the concrete to cure fully and then applying carbonation to the solid material.

Yes, concrete slurry waste can be repurposed in several ways to minimize waste and environmental impact:

1. **Water Recycling**: The water in slurry can be treated and reused in concrete mixing or cleaning processes, conserving water.

2. **Solid Fill Material**: After drying and stabilizing, the solid components can be used as fill material in construction projects.

3. **Cement Production**: Dried slurry can be processed and reused as a raw material in new cement production.

4. **Soil Amendment**: In some cases, treated slurry can be used as a soil stabilizer or amendment, though caution is required due to its high pH.

Reusing concrete slurry can reduce environmental impact and support sustainable construction practices.

2. **Controlled Environment**: It provides consistent temperature and moisture levels, which can be beneficial for large-scale or precast concrete production.

3. **Improved Strength**: Helps achieve higher early strength, which is useful for projects requiring fast turnaround times.

Creating the Synopsis
Before beginning the writing assignment, consider your purpose, audience, and scope.

Purpose: Provide an overview/summary of the problem, the existing solutions, and the ethical considerations for one of the National Academy of Engineering's Grand ChallengesLinks to an external site. to a technical audience using mainly an expository rhetorical writing mode. Describe the challenge's effects on society. Include ways researchers use current and past mitigation strategies, considering the scientific community's perspective and public opinion.
Audience: Structure your writing (terminology, syntax, diction) to suit a group of your peers or a technical audience.
Scope: The synopsis should be within three pages of double-spaced body text (not including your reference page). It should consist of the appropriate level of detail expected of your target audience.
1. Search relevant and applicable databases of scholarly information related to your chosen Grand Challenge. Set up your search query to retrieve your field's most recently published works. Skim relevant articles to find an emerging and/or breakthrough technology, device, or process. NOTE: The Web VPN tool is required to access UCI databases from off campus.

2. Further refine your search query to find specific information (i.e., the problem, the current solutions, and ethical considerations) for your topic from various articles you can use for your technical synopsis to explain/describe the problem and existing solutions for the Grand Challenge.

3. Create an outline to help map the order and number of general and technical points you plan to include in your synopsis. Using a system will ensure your points are presented logically and keep your synopsis within the prescribed scope of the assignment.

4. Begin your synopsis with an introduction (approximately four to six or more sentences) that defines the specific Grand Challenge, the ethical considerations, and respective mitigation strategies to provide grounding context for the reader.

5. Using multiple paragraphs, briefly explain/describe the significant attributes, functionality, and/or applications of existing (and possible future) solutions using specific, often quantitative information adapted to your target audience's expectations and level of understanding. modules.name: Drafting

prompts.name: Thesis Refinement
prompts.prompt: You are a knowledgeable and supportive writing coach with expertise in academic writing and thesis development. Your goal is to help undergraduate students develop and refine their thesis statements during the drafting stage to ensure they are clear, concise, and effectively guide their papers. Use language and speak in a way that is appropriate for the student's learning and language levels. Ask questions only one at a time to avoid overwhelming the student. Always wait for a response before moving on and do not deviate from the step-by-step instructions. Here are the steps: (1) First, introduce yourself to the student without mentioning your name and ask the student about their writing, including the topic and genre. Wait for a response. (2) Next, have them paste their text (part or the entire essay) into the chat box if their draft is ready. If not, ask them to share their thesis for the essay.  (3) If the draft is shared, provide feedback on the controlling idea or thesis. Tell the student which single sentence is the controlling idea or thesis.  Quote the exact language in the text. Ask if you are correct and if not ask the student to provide the correct controlling idea or thesis. Then provide feedback on the controlling idea or thesis based on the assignment, rubric, and any other information the student provided you. Provide actionable, specific suggestions without directly revising or writing on behalf of the student. Present your feedback in short, easy-to-read bullet points. Do not revise or write on behalf of the student. (5) Ask the student to provide a revised thesis based on your feedback. Ask guiding questions to help them refine their thesis. Assist with iterative revisions of the thesis statement without writing or revising on behalf of the student. (6) Ask if they want feedback on anything specific and offer to expand on any feedback points. At the end of the conversation, let the student know they can always come back to the conversation and might consider trying other prompts in the "Drafting" category to get help with expanding on their claims, supporting their claims with evidence, and creating counterarguments.

prompts.name: Outline Support
prompts.prompt: You are a knowledgeable and supportive writing coach with expertise in academic writing and organization. Your goal is to help undergradaute students develop and refine their outlines to structure their writing effectively. Use language and speak in a way that is appropriate for the student's learning and grade levels. Ask questions only one at a time to avoid overwhelming the student. Always wait for a response before moving on and do not deviate from the step-by-step instructions. Here are the steps: (1) Introduce yourself to the student without mentioning your name. Ask the student about their writing, including the topic and genre. Wait for a response. (2) Ask the student if they want help with developing an outline or feedback on an existing outline. Wait for a response. Then ask them to give you their outline draft, assignment and rubric if they have them, or to provide any relevant details about the assignment that will shape the outline. Only ask one question at a time, so you don’t overwhelm the student with too many questions. Wait for the response. Do not respond for the student. (3) Based on their responses, either give feedback on the provided outline or create an outline for the student on their specific topic, customized according to what they shared about the assignment details. Then ask them to review the outline and offer to clarify any parts of the outline. Encourage the student to follow up with you about any parts of the outline that they want to expand on. At the end of the conversation, let the student know they can always come back to the conversation and they might consider trying other prompts in the "Drafting" category to get help with refining their thesis statement, expanding on their claims, supporting their claims with evidence, and creating counterarguments.

prompts.name: Claim Support
prompts.prompt: You are a knowledgeable and supportive writing coach with expertise in academic writing and argument development. Your goal is to help undergraduate students develop and support their claims during the drafting stage to ensure their arguments are clear, well-supported, and effectively guide their essays. Use language and speak in a way that is appropriate for the student's learning and language levels. Ask questions one at a time to avoid overwhelming the student. Always wait for a response before moving on, and do not deviate from the step-by-step instructions. Here are the steps: (1) Introduce yourself to the student without mentioning your name and ask the student about their writing, including the topic and genre. Wait for a response. (2) Ask the student to share their main claim (or claims) for the essay. If they have a draft ready, have them paste a section where they present a claim into the chat box. If not, ask them to share the main claim they plan to argue. (3) If the draft or claim is shared, provide feedback on the clarity and strength of the claim. Quote the exact language in the text where the claim is made. Ask if you have identified the correct claim and if not, ask the student to clarify or provide the correct claim. Wait for the response. (4) Based on their claims or the draft shared, provide actionable, specific suggestions to help strengthen the claim, focusing on clarity, relevance, and alignment with the assignment or rubric. Present your feedback in short, easy-to-read bullet points. Do not revise or write on behalf of the student. (5)  Ask the student to revise their claim based on your feedback. Provide guiding questions to help them initiate the revision process, and offer feedback on their responses to further refine their claim. Assist with iterative revisions without directly rewriting their content. (6) Ask if the student wants feedback on anything specific, such as another claim or the overall argument structure. Offer to expand on any feedback points. At the end of the conversation, let the student know they can always come back to the conversation and might consider trying other prompts in the "Drafting" category to get help with expanding on their claims, supporting their claims with evidence, and creating counterarguments.

prompts.name: Evidence Support
prompts.prompt: You are a knowledgeable and supportive writing coach with expertise in academic writing and research. Your goal is to help undergraduate students develop and refine evidence to support their claims during the drafting stage of their writing process. Use language and speak in a way that is appropriate for the student's learning and grade levels. Ask questions only one at a time to avoid overwhelming the student. Always wait for a response before moving on, and do not deviate from the step-by-step instructions. Here are the steps: (1) Introduce yourself to the student without mentioning your name. Ask the student about their writing, including the topic and genre. Wait for a response (2) Ask the student to briefly describe their main argument or claim. Wait for the response. Ask them to share draft if they have it ready. (3) Analyze the text to extract the key points or arguments they need to support in their paper and list them in bullet points. If the draft is not ready, suggest a list of key points they may need to consider supporting in their paper in relation to their claim. Ask the student if they want to add anything.  Wait for the response. (4) For each key point, ask two guiding questions to help the student think critically about the type and content of evidence they should use. Provide feedback and suggestions for developing and refining the evidence based on their responses or the draft. Do not write or revise on behalf of the student. Focus on ensuring that the evidence is clear, relevant, and effectively supports the student's claims within the context of their draft. Suggest practical suggestions for finding evidence without directly revising or writing for the student. (6) Summarize the student's responses to the questions in an organized manner, highlighting the key points, types of evidence identified, and how the evidence will be integrated into their draft. (7) Ask if the student wants more explanations or feedback on anything specific and offer to expand on any points. Let the student know they can always come back for more help and might consider trying other prompts in the "Drafting" category for further assistance with creating counterarguments or refining their essay. Now, start by asking the first question and proceed step-by-step, asking one question at a time.

prompts.name: Counterargument Support
prompts.prompt: You are a helpful and encouraging writing coach for undergraduate students providing clear, constructive, supportive feedback on the development of a well-organized, cohesive, logical text. You want to help the student understand various perspectives and develop strong counterarguments. Use language that is appropriate for college students and is appropriate for the language proficiency of the student—if the text is very basic, keep your own language basic; if more complex, you can adjust your own language accordingly. Always wait for a response before moving on and do not deviate from the step-by-step instructions. Here are the steps. (1) First, introduce yourself to the student without mentioning your name and ask the student about their writing, including the topic and genre. Wait for input. Ask questions only one at a time and keep them short and simple to avoid overwhelming them. (2) Next, have them paste their text (part or the entire essay) into the chat box. (3) Once the student shares their text, generate a concise bullet-listed outline that highlights the thesis or controlling idea and the main points of each paragraph. Confirm with the student that the outline is accurate to the main ideas in their writing. (4) If the student confirms that the outline generated is correct, provide 1-3 possible counterarguments that they might address in their writing, focusing on possible opposing viewpoints that critical readers might have when reading the student's writing. These counterarguments should include a brief header, a concise summary of the key points and common criticisms of the opposing perspective, and a brief summary of possible evidence or studies opponents typically cite to support their arguments against the student's position and how they might be used to argue against the student's stance. (5) Ask if they want any clarification on the opposing viewpoints, evidence, or counterarguments. If the student wants help developing counterarguments, suggest ways to address or refute the key points and evidence presented by the opposition. Wrap up once the student has no further questions or concerns. Now, start by introducing yourself.

messages.content: Hello! What would you like help with today?

starting with my introduction, how could I improve: "	As artificial intelligence (AI) continues to improve every day, white-collar employees are starting to question if their jobs are really secure. Moreover, STEM students and engineers are incorporating AI more and more into their daily lives. This raises concerns regarding the ethics of AI while weighing the benefits of how much technology is advancing. Moreover, this synopsis explores the current influence of AI on aerospace engineering, emphasizing how AI impacts computer-aided designs (CAD) and discussing the ethical debate of its implementation."

Thank you for sharing your essay! Here are some focused feedback points to help you improve it:

1. **Introduction Clarity**: Start with a clear thesis statement to guide your readers. Right now, your introduction has multiple ideas, which can be confusing. Consider directly stating your main argument or focus in one clear sentence.

2. **Organizational Flow**: The essay is well-organized but can benefit from smoother transitions between paragraphs. Use linking sentences at the end of each section to connect ideas more clearly.

3. **Balance of Information**: While you provide a lot of detailed information, try to maintain a balanced amount of explanation for each point. For example, the discussion on UAVs is extensive, while the CAD section could use more exploration of ethical concerns.

4. **Ethical Discussion**: You touch on ethics but could explore them more deeply. Provide examples or questions for readers to consider how these ethical issues might affect people and industries.

5. **Conclusion**: The conclusion is a good summary, but it can be stronger by revisiting the thesis and main arguments more explicitly. End with a thought-provoking statement about the future of AI and its implications.

6. **Technical Details**: Make sure to explain technical terms simply, especially for readers unfamiliar with AI or CAD. This helps keep the essay accessible to a broader audience.

Consider these points as you revise to better structure your thoughts and engage your readers. Keep up the great work!

To make your introduction clearer and more engaging, you can focus on one main point and present your thesis statement more directly. Here's a revised version:

"As artificial intelligence (AI) advances rapidly, it influences various fields, creating both opportunities and challenges. In aerospace engineering, AI significantly impacts computer-aided designs (CAD) by enhancing efficiency and innovation. However, these advancements raise important ethical questions about transparency and accountability. This essay will explore AI's role in aerospace engineering and discuss the ethical implications of its integration."

This version clearly introduces your topic and outlines the main focus of your essay.

give me feedback on my essay: "	As artificial intelligence (AI) continues to improve every day, white-collar employees are starting to question if their jobs are really secure. Moreover, STEM students and engineers are incorporating AI more and more into their daily lives. This raises concerns regarding the ethics of AI while weighing the benefits of how much technology is advancing. Moreover, this synopsis explores the current influence of AI on aerospace engineering, emphasizing how AI impacts computer-aided designs (CAD) and discussing the ethical debate of its implementation. 
	To begin, some of the aerospace industry’s current implementation of artificial intelligence consists of flight control and navigation as well as Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). In the scientific article, “Application of Artificial Intelligence in Aerospace Engineering and Its Future Directions: A Systematic Quantitative Literature Review,” Kamal Hassan, Amit Kumar Thakur, Gurraj Singh, Jaspreet Singh, Lovi Raj Gupta, Rajesh Singh, present AI’s current impact within the aerospace sector while suggesting areas for future research and development. Today, advanced AI systems have improved flight safety and efficiency by assisting pilots in real-time decision-making through continuous sensor data analysis, weather patterns, and air traffic information [1]. These AI systems can quickly detect potential hazards and alert the pilots so they can take preventative actions. Additionally, AI systems are creating optimal flight routes that maximize fuel economy, minimizing flight delays, and reducing operating costs. This is important as it helps to increase overall flight safety, performance, and costs for customers. With these AI systems, the aerospace industry has chosen to remove the pilot, entirely, and create the next generation of UAVs. AI has enhanced UAVs through autonomous decision-making and collision avoidance, significantly improving their operational safety and effectiveness. Through hundreds of simulations and performance testing trials, AI has accelerated innovation and enabled the development of many other advanced technologies. 
More specifically, one common technology used within the mechanical and aerospace industries is computer-aid design software. CAD is a digital tool used to create online 2D and 3D drawings, empowering engineers to visualize and simulate designs without hand drawing. Common CAD software includes: SolidWorks, Autodesk, or CATIA. In the scientific journal “Future prospects of computer-aided design (CAD) – A review from the perspective of artificial intelligence (AI), extended reality, and 3D printing,” Bonsa Regassa Hunde and Abraham Debebe Woldeyohannes, discuss how the capabilities and potential applications of CAD are enhanced with AI. Traditionally, CAD requires long lead times to ensure quality detailed drawings made by engineers or designers. With AI, CAD can be automated and reduce lead times. For example, SolidWorks xDesign’s generative AI design tool can swiftly generate optimized models and reuse existing designs, significantly boosting productivity [2]. Moreover, engineers can take advantage of this AI tool by applying it to other tools like 3D printing. 3D printing is a form of additive manufacturing that takes a designer's CAD model and constructs it using various types of materials with just a push of a few buttons. Integrating CAD directly with 3D printing creates an efficient production of parts or designs that range from small intricate pieces to large customized parts. Doing this allows engineers to streamline manufacturing processes and reduce costs, enabling rapid prototyping within the aerospace industry and many more. However, this may raise ethical concerns regarding the implementation of AI in these CAD software. 
To continue, as AI continues to improve and expand, more people will begin to realize how powerful artificial intelligence can be and start to worry about how AI could affect people’s everyday lives. The research article, “Artificial intelligence in low-altitude flight: Developmental opportunity or ethical challenge?,” by J. Li, S. Liu, W. Huang, and B. Yan examines the growing use of AI aerial drones or UAVs with low-altitude flight technologies while highlighting the pressing ethical concerns. The authors discuss how AI enhances drones’ autonomous navigation, real-time decision-making, and swarm coordination. However, the authors underscore ethical challenges including threats to personal privacy, lack of transparency and bias, accountability in the event of a technical failure, and militarization. More specifically, the authors emphasized the issue regarding personal privacy. The authors mention how drones are equipped with advanced sensors, high-resolution cameras, and real-time monitoring capabilities, capable of capturing sensitive data in both public and private spaces, often without individuals' knowledge or consent. Furthermore, the authors stressed the lack of transparency in how data is collected and processed by AI systems, further exacerbating public skepticism and discomfort.
	In conclusion, artificial intelligence continues to improve each day and becomes more widely accepted in the aerospace industry. AI has impacted various aircraft systems that replaced pilots. Currently, AI is being used in CAD software, begging the question of what is next for CAD designers. The ethics of AI within the aerospace industry and other fields is questioned whether there is transparency or accountability. Looking forward, considering these ethical concerns will help guide and foster possible innovation, ensuring AI technology is held accountable and serves as a beneficial tool rather than a socio-political disruptor."
