messages.content: is this article a good length for a 2 to 3 page summary

does the article also fit all of the requirements listed here: The assignment aims to review and summarize an article from a scientific journal (e.g., IEEE Xplore, Journal of Fluid Mechanics, Energy and Environment, EE Times, etc.) published in the last ten years for a generalist (non-technical) audience. You may optionally choose to review and summarize a conference paper. The selected article should be related to engineering and suitable for creating a 2 to 3-page summary (3-page maximum for the body text) using double-spaced, left-justified text. A lengthy article will be challenging to summarize within the scope of the assignment. A highly specialized article will be challenging to summarize for a generalist (non-technical) audience.

THEME ARTICLE: MICROPROCESSOR AT 50  Evolution of the Graphics Processing Unit (GPU)  William J. Dally   and Stephen W. Keckler,   NVIDIA Corporation, Santa Clara, CA, 95051, USA  David B. Kirk   ,   Independent Consultant  G raphics processing units (GPUs) power today ’ s fastest   supercomputers,   are   the   dominant platform for deep learning, and provide the intelligence for devices ranging from self-driving cars to robots and smart cameras. They also generate com- pelling photorealistic images at real-time frame rates. GPUs have evolved by adding features to support new use cases. NVIDIA ’ s GeForce 256, the   fi rst GPU, was a dedicated processor for real-time graphics, an appli- cation that demands large amounts of   fl oating-point arithmetic for vertex and fragment shading computa- tions and high memory bandwidth. As real-time graphics advanced, GPUs became programmable. The combina- tion of programmability and   fl oating-point performance made GPUs attractive for running scienti fi c applications. Scientists found ways to use early programmable GPUs by casting their calculations as vertex and fragment  shaders . GPUs evolved to meet the needs of scienti fi c users by adding hardware for simpler programming, dou- ble-precision   fl oating-point arithmetic, and resilience. The availability of easily programmed GPUs with high   fl oating-point performance enabled the current revolution in deep learning. AI researchers have found them the ideal platforms to train deep neural net- works for vision, speech recognition, natural language processing, recommender systems, and other applica- tions. GPUs evolved to meet the needs of these appli- cations by adding deep-learning-speci fi c data types and instructions. Today, they are the dominant plat- form for deep learning training and inference.  EARLY 3-D GRAPHICS HARDWARE  The high computational demands of graphics ren- dering   led   to   the   development   of   special-purpose hardware   to   carry   out   the   computations   needed to   generate   an   image.   The   Evans   and   Sutherland Computer Corporation was founded in 1968 to build special-purpose   3-D   graphics   hardware.   Using   the small-scale integration technology of the day, these expensive multirack systems were used for demand- ing applications such as   fl ight simulators.  THE AVAILABILITY OF EASILY PROGRAMMED GPUs WITH HIGH FLOATING-POINT PERFORMANCE ENABLED THE CURRENT REVOLUTION IN DEEP LEARNING.  Moore ’ s law scaling of integrated circuit technol- ogy drastically reduced the cost of graphics systems and led them to evolve in two directions. In the late 1980s, Silicon Graphics built a new generation of 3-D graphics systems and high-end professional worksta- tions. At the same time, low-cost 2-D graphics capabil- ities were supported both in mass market products such as PCs and in video game consoles. Early con- soles were sprite-based, copying virtual rectangles to the screen to display backgrounds, scenes, and char- acters. The earliest PC graphics cards were simply 2-D accelerators and display controllers. They copied pixel values   from   PC   system   memory   to   frame   buffer memory, and ultimately to a CRT screen generating addresses and sync signals and providing D/A conver- sion. 2-D graphics accelerators evolved to support text and window acceleration in hardware, copying, stretching, and blending images (textures) together. The advent of 3-D video game consoles such as 3DO, Sega Saturn, and Sony Playstation ushered in the era of mass-market 3-D gaming. The desire to support 3-D graphics on PCs for gaming inspired the entire   fi eld of 2.5-D and 3-D graphics accelerators, an opportunity so compelling that over 60 companies were founded to address this market. Founded in 1993 by Jensen Huang, Chris Malachowsky, and Curtis Priem, NVIDIA aimed to bring sophisticated 3-D graphics to the 0272-1732   ß   2021 IEEE Digital Object Identi fi er 10.1109/MM.2021.3113475 Date of current version 19 November 2021.  IEEE Micro   Published by the IEEE Computer Society   November/December 2021 42 Authorized licensed use limited to: Access paid by The UC Irvine Libraries. Downloaded on April 09,2025 at 06:24:17 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. PC platform and to compete with game consoles. The inspiration for 3-D PC graphics came from the sophisti- cated and expensive graphics pipelines in 3-D profes- sional workstations such as those created by Silicon Graphics. NVIDIA ’ s founders observed that the larger and more complex chips enabled by Moore ’ s law would allow increased integration and functionality to make PC-based 3-D gaming affordable. The   fi rst generation of 3-D PC graphics cards per- formed fragment (or pixel) computations of rasteriza- tion, color interpolation, texture mapping, Z-buffering (hidden   surface   removal),   and   shading.   Given   the coordinates and parameters of a triangle ’ s vertices in a 2-D screen space, they would generate the depth and color of each pixel in the triangle and composite these pixels into the frame buffer. The vertex compu- tations to transform the vertices from world space to screen space, clip the triangles against the viewport, and compute the lighting of each vertex remained on the host CPU. NVIDIA ’ s   fi rst successful product of this type was RIVA-128 (NV3), which was released in 1997. The evolution of these 3-D accelerators included the ability to blend multiple textures simultaneously for more sophisticated lighting and shading. NVIDIA ’ s RIVA-TNT and TNT2 (NV4 and NV5) had these capabili- ties, as well as parallel processing of multiple pixels at several points in the pipeline, including rasterization, shading, and at the Z-buffer/memory interface.  GeForce 256: THE FIRST GPU  Introduced in 1999, the GeForce 256 was the   fi rst chip to combine the vertex computations for transforma- tion and lighting and the fragment computations on the same chip. This fully integrated graphics engine was named a   “ graphics processing unit ”   or GPU. Off- loading   the   vertex   computations   from   the   host enabled higher geometric complexity in games at the cost   of   requiring   signi fi cant   fl oating-point   perfor- mance. For example, the perspective transformation requires a 4  4 matrix – vector multiply and a perspec- tive division operation. The vertex and pixel computa- tions in the GeForce 256 were con fi gurable   fi xed- function hardware, organized in a hardwired pipeline. As PC games became more sophisticated, demand grew to modify the vertex and pixel computations to achieve more complex graphical effects. Programma- ble   “ shaders ”   had   been   used   in   of fl ine   software graphics since the 1980s. The GeForce 3 (NV20) GPU introduced a programmable vertex shader in 2001, and in 2002, the GeForce FX introduced programmable fragment shaders.  GPU COMPUTING  As the combination of high   fl oating-point performance and programmability made GPUs with programmable shaders an attractive platform for scienti fi c computing, the   fi eld of general-purpose GPU (GPGPU) program- ming emerged. A GeForce 6 had a peak performance of 108 billion single-precision   fl oating-point operations per second (108 GFLOPS), in contrast to a contempo- rary CPU that provided only 8 GFLOPS. However, pro- gramming these   GPUs was   challenging.   Input and output were very restricted. Fragment shaders only received input from interpolated vertex attributes and textures and only deposited output into the frame buffer. Programmers had to cast their applications as rendering texture mapped and blended triangles to harness the FLOPS of these GPUs. Early GPGPU pro- grams contained pages of inscrutable shader code that forced scienti fi c algorithms into this form. To harness the increasing capabilities of the hard- ware, GPUs ultimately leveraged stream processing pro- gramming   models   that   hid   much   of   the   complexity of shader programming from scienti fi c programmers. Emphasizing parallelism and producer – consumer locality, stream programming systems were originally developed for dedicated stream processors such as Imagine 1   and Merrimac. 2   These systems, however, were ideally suited to GPU programming. The Brook programming language, originally developed for Merrimac, was adapted to GPUs as Brook-GPU. 3   Brook was later adopted by AMD as the language used to program their uni fi ed shader GPUs. The stream processing model in fl uenced the design of GPUs intended for computing. Starting in 2003, Bill Dally, leader of the stream processing project at Stan- ford, began consulting with NVIDIA working with John Nickolls and Erik Lindholm to transfer stream process- ing ideas into the design of the GeForce 8 (NV50) GPU. In 2004, Ian Buck, the principal developer of Brook, joined NVIDIA and with many collaborators developed the CUDA programming language. 4 The GeForce 8 GPU or G80 introduced streaming multiprocessors (SMs) that were used to run both ver- tex and fragment shaders. These SMs could also run  “ compute shaders ”   independent of the graphics pipeline speci fi cally in the form of CUDA cooperative thread arrays. A shared memory facilitated communication between the threads in an array. The G80   “ Tesla ”   GPU and CUDA were launched in 2006, and they greatly reduced the barriers to developing GPU-based scienti fi c and   other   general-purpose   applications. 5,6   Figure   1 shows a die photo of a G80 GPU, which consists of 681 million transistors and delivers 346 GFLOPS of FP32 throughput and 86 GB/s of DRAM bandwidth.  November/December 2021   IEEE Micro   43 MICROPROCESSOR AT 50  Authorized licensed use limited to: Access paid by The UC Irvine Libraries. Downloaded on April 09,2025 at 06:24:17 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. However, revolutionary hardware and a new pro- gramming language do not by themselves make a revo- lution.   Legacy   high-performance   computing   (HPC) applications were primarily written in FORTRAN and were single-threaded serial programs. G80 was a mas- sively parallel threaded processor, and CUDA was a thread- and data-parallel C-like programming language. David Kirk, NVIDIA ’ s Chief Scientist, and Wen-mei Hwu, Professor of Electrical and Computer Engineering at University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign (UIUC), pro- totyped a graduate class at UIUC to produce a new gen- eration of parallel programmers. The lecture notes from this class evolved into the popular parallel programming textbook   Programming Massively Parallel Processors , 7 which has been translated into many languages and printings, and will soon be released in its 4th edition. At the same time, NVIDIA funded support for CUDA Centers of Excellence at many universities to promote the teaching of CUDA and adoption of GPU technology and parallel programming. Kirk and Hwu also supported many   “ teach the teachers ”   events including the annual PUMPS summer school at UPC/BSC in Barcelona, Spain. Feedback from the large and growing GPU comput- ing community led to the development of HPC-speci fi c features in the Fermi generation of GPUs in 2010. These features included improved support for double-preci- sion   fl oating-point operations, 750 GFLOPS DP in Fermi versus zero in G80, and reliability, availability, and ser- viceability (RAS) features including ECC on main mem- ory and parity protection for on-chip memories. With HPC features, high performance, and ef fi - ciency, GPUs quickly became the technology of choice for large scienti fi c computers. A major milestone for GPU computing was the Titan system consisting of 18,688   NVIDIA   Kepler   (K20)   GPUs   at   Oak   Ridge National Labs (ORNL), which took the #1 position on the Top 500 list in November 2012 with a Linpack per- formance of 17.6 PFLOPS. In June 2018, the Summit System at ORNL continued the tradition, taking the #1 position with a Linpack performance of 122.3 PFLOPS, powered by 27,648 NVIDIA Volta (V100) GPUs.   8   Six of the top ten machines on the June, 2021 Top 500 list were powered by NVIDIA GPUs.  THE CURRENT REVOLUTION IN DEEP LEARNING WAS ENABLED BY GPUs , AND FURTHER PROGRESS IN DEEP LEARNING IS LARGELY GATED BY GPU PERFORMANCE.  The success of GPUs in HPC has required the devel- opment of a large and diverse software ecosystem, whose maturation took place over the course of a decade. Based on the CUDA programming system, the GPU programming ecosystem now includes other meth- ods of programming GPUs, such as CUDA Fortran and OpenACC. The next level of support is a set of numerical libraries including CuBLAS, CuSparse, and CuFFT that provide highly optimized code for the key functions of many numerical programs. Applications can then lever- age these libraries to exploit GPU capabilities. Today, over 600 HPC applications are accelerated by GPUs, 9 including molecular dynamics codes such as GROMACS, NAMD, AMBER, and LAMMPS; weather codes such as WRF;   fl uid dynamics codes such as ANSYS and Open- FOAM; chemistry codes such as Gaussian, VASP, Quan- tum Espresso, and GAMESS; and structural analysis codes such as LS-DYNA and ANSYS.  DEEP LEARNING HARDWARE  The current revolution in deep learning was enabled by GPUs, and further progress in deep learning is largely gated by GPU performance. Making deep learn- ing successful has required three ingredients: algo- rithms, datasets, and hardware. The core algorithms for deep learning: deep neural networks, convolutional networks, training with backpropagation, stochastic gradient descent, and others, had all been developed in the 1980s or earlier. Large datasets such as PASCAL VOC and ImageNet were available by 2005. The miss- ing   ingredient   was   hardware   fast   enough   to   train  FIGURE 1.   NVIDIA G80 GPU launched November, 2006. This ingredient was provided by easily programma- ble GPUs with high   fl oating-point performance. The  fi eld of computer vision, for example, was transformed by the development of AlexNet in 2012, a convolutional neural network trained on a pair of GeForce 580 (Fermi) GPUs in two weeks. AlexNet won the ImageNet com- petition in 2012 with such a large accuracy improve- ment   that   the   computer   vision   community   largely abandoned manually designed feature detectors in favor of neural networks. Similar results followed in other   fi elds such as speech recognition, natural lan- guage processing, and recommender systems. GPUs   democratized   deep   learning,   making   the technology accessible to anyone with a GPU-equipped PC. In 2010, researchers at Google Brain built a deep neural network to   fi nd cats on the Internet using 16,000 CPUs. This experiment was repeated in 2012 on 48   GPUs 10   using   software   that   ultimately   became cuDNN, a CUDA library for deep learning. Just as CUDA simpli fi ed the task of scienti fi c programming on GPUs, cuDNN simpli fi ed the task of implementing deep learning on GPUs, facilitating widespread use by deep learning researchers and practitioners. As shown in Figure 2, the complexity of deep learn- ing models has increased dramatically over time. The complexity of natural language models, for example, has increased by 1,000    in only two years from BERT in 2018 to GPT-3 in 2020. These models have signi fi cantly better   fi delity, but the ability to train successively more complex models has depended on increasing hardware performance. GPUs have evolved to meet this rapidly growing demand for deep learning training performance in two ways: scale-up and scale out. To facilitate scale-out, in 2015, the Pascal GPU introduced NVLink, a high-speed GPU – GPU communication channel. Then, in 2017, the Volta GPU introduced NVSwitch to enable high-perfor- mance networks of GPUs. Nodes of GPUs connected by NVSwitch share memory and communicate with other nodes via In fi niband networking. While AlexNet was origi- nally trained on two GPUs, modern language models are trained on clusters with many thousands of GPUs. For example, Selene, which is #6 on the June 2021 Top 500 list, is a deep learning (DL) training cluster consisting of 560 DGX-A100 boxes connected by In fi niband. Each DGX box contains eight A100 GPUs connected by NVSwitch for a total of 4,480 GPUs. Figure 3 shows how single GPU performance has scaled up to meet the demands of deep learning. GPU inference performance has improved by 317  , more than doubling each year, from 2012 (Kepler) to 2020 (Ampere), a trend far exceeding what Moore ’ s law scaling would predict. Dubbed Huang ’ s Law, this increase has been largely due to the addition of DL-speci fi c data types and instructions. 11   The   inference   arithmetic   performance available in Kepler was 4 TFLOPS of 32-bit   fl oating-point (FP32) operations. This data representation exceeds the precision required for DL (particularly for inference). In addition, as the most complex instruction available in Kepler was a fused multiply – add (FMA), Kepler could only amortize the overhead of instruction fetch, decode, and operand fetch over two math operations per lane. a   In 2016, the Pascal architecture increased FP32 perfor- mance to 10.6 TFLOPS and also added 21.3 TFLOPS  FIGURE 2. Pascal not only delivered higher DL performance but did so more ef fi ciently because: 1) the accuracy and dynamic range of FP16 is adequate for most   DL   applications;   and   2)   Pascal ’ s   dot-product instructions, like FDP4, amortize the instruction overhead over additional arithmetic operations. In 2017, Volta introduced a major advance in DL per- formance with the   Tensor Core   architecture. The Volta Tensor Cores implement a 4  4 FP16 matrix multiply and accumulate instruction, HMMA, which forms the inner loop of most DL algorithms. This single instruc- tion implements 128   fl oating-point operations (64 FP16 multiplies and 64 FP32 adds), which amortizes instruc- tion overheads and makes them negligible. As a result, the GPU Tensor Cores deliver the   fl exibility of a pro- grammable architecture with the ef fi ciency of a spe- cialized DL chip. The FP16 tensor cores in Volta provide an aggregate 125 TFLOPS of FP16 performance. In 2018, Turing introduced integer tensor cores, which provide 260 TOPS of INT8 performance through an IMMA instruction that performs an 8  8 INT8 matrix multiply and accumulate. This support in Turing was ideal for most inference tasks for which INT8 has suf fi cient accuracy and dynamic range. In 2020, Ampere introduced tensor core support for computation on sparse data. These sparse MMA instructions double performance for weight matrices where two out of every four elements are zero, called  structured   sparsity .   The   sparse   instructions   bring Ampere ’ s   total   inference   performance   to   1,248 TOPS (INT8). Ampere is described in more detail in in the next section. As with HPC, software is a key component of the success of GPUs in deep learning. Starting with the CuDNN library in 2012, a large ecosystem of deep learning software has been developed to aid in the creation and deployment of machine learning applica- tions. NVIDIA ’ s Tensor RT (TRT) optimizes inference code and its Triton Inference Server manages schedul- ing of multiple models. Third-party frameworks includ- ing TensorFlow, PyTorch, and Caffe2 are supported. The DALI library manages the parts of training pipe- lines auxiliary to deep learning, such as staging and decompressing the data. A template library called CUTLASS simpli fi es the process of developing highly optimized matrix multiply kernels for each new gener- ation of GPUs. Libraries supporting applications such as medical imaging and video analytics are available. The evolution of software stacks is also critical to DL performance. In MLPerf Training v1.0 submissions, the performance of benchmarks improved by up to 2.1   on the same hardware, due to software enhance- ments alone. Because of their high performance and rich soft- ware   ecosystem,   today ’ s   GPUs   are   the   dominant platform for deep learning applications. The most recent   MLPerf   results   demonstrate   that   NVIDIA GPU systems are the fastest commercially available platforms   for   both   datacenter   inference   and training.   12 – 14  BECAUSE OF THEIR HIGH PERFORMANCE AND RICH SOFTWARE ECOSYSTEM, TODAY ’ S GPUs ARE THE DOMINANT PLATFORM FOR DEEP LEARNING APPLICATIONS. A100: TODAY ’ S GPU  The Ampere A100, launched in 2020 and shown in Figure 4, is the most recent NVIDIA GPU.   15   Ampere is built using a 7-nm technology and includes 54 billion transistors on a reticle-limited 826-mm   2   die. Table 1 compares key attributes of Ampere A100 (2020) to its predecessor in the datacenter, the Volta V100 (2017) GPU. Ampere dramatically increases each of these key   hardware   features,   including   5    greater   FP16 throughput, 2.2    more DRAM bandwidth, and 6.7   more on-chip L2 cache. In addition to massive parallel computing throughput and memory bandwidth, the Ampere architecture includes hardware support for accelerating machine learning and HPC applications along with features for scalability to large datacenters and supercomputers. Ampere also provides support for advances in the CUDA   programming   model.   For   example,   Ampere accelerates   CUDA   task   graphs   by   automatically launching dependent compute tasks within complex task graphs. Ampere also provides hardware support for   arrive – wait   barriers   that   facilitate   coordination of asynchronous tasks in the GPU. Finally, Ampere provides   additional   hardware   support   for   CUDA ’ s Cooperative Groups, such as a warp-wide reduction operation   that   accelerates   group-wide   collective operations.  Machine Learning Features  Each Ampere Tensor Core employs its 256 FP16   fl oat- ing-point units to perform an 8  4  8 mixed-precision matrix multiply every cycle. The Tensor Core architec- ture also supports high-throughput computation for different numerical representations, including binary (INT1), INT4, INT8, FP16, and BFloat16 (8-bit exponent, 7-bit mantissa or E8:M7). A100 also introduces a new TensorFloat-32 (TF32) format (E8:M10) that provides the   same   exponent   dynamic   range   as   FP32   but reduces the precision of the mantissa to 10 bits. A100 provides hardware for this 19-bit format that runs at 8    the throughput of FP32 but with suf fi cient preci- sion to mimic the accuracy of DNNs trained with FP32.   b Mentioned earlier, A100 also provides hardware support for structured sparsity in the Tensor Cores that can be used to dramatically reduce DNN weight storage and boost throughput by eliminating unneces- sary arithmetic. Structured sparsity can be applied to compress the weight matrix (typically used as matrix A in the C = A  B GEMM formulation in the Tensor Cores) by requiring two of the four values in each subvector to be zero. This feature transforms the computation into a smaller matrix that requires half the operations of a dense formulation, producing a 2    speedup. While structured sparsity requires an additional neural net- work training re fi nement step, it produces no loss in accuracy on a wide range of networks including those for vision, object detection, segmentation, natural lan- guage modeling, and language translation. In the memory system, A100 provides a range of fea- tures to provide better control over data movement and placement. To improve ef fi ciency, A100 supports data transfers directly from the memory hierarchy into shared memory, without requiring the data to transit through the register   fi le. A100 also provides a new set of L2 cache control operations that allow the programmer  TABLE 1.   V100 and A100 speci fi cations. HPC Features  Traditional HPC workloads continue to demand more double-precision   compute   performance,   memory bandwidth, and interconnect bandwidth. Leveraging architecture   concepts   for   GEMM   acceleration,   the A100 incorporates Tensor Core support for double- precision FP64 data types, boosting peak GPU perfor- mance to 19.5 TFLOPS. In addition, A100 deploys the third generation of NVLink interconnect, delivering 600 GB/s of total off-chip bandwidth through a combi- nation of 50 Gb/s pin data rates and 12 NVLink chan- nels.   This   high-speed   interconnect   enables   tightly coupled systems such as the DGX A100 to include eight fully connected GPUs that can sustain 600 GB/s between all pairs of GPUs and deliver 150 TFLOPS of double-precision performance. When extended with In fi niband,   the   560   DGX-A100   systems   of   Selene deliver 64 PFLOPS in just 2.6 MW; nine of the ten most energy-ef fi cient   supercomputers   on   the   June   2021 Green 500 list are based on A100 GPUs.  Graphics Features  In addition to traditional raster graphics, the Ampere architecture includes improved hardware support for ray tracing. Introduced in the Turing GPU generation, the NVIDIA RT cores are integrated with the GPU ’ s SMs to accelerate bounding-volume hierarchy data structure   traversal   and   ray-triangle   intersection tests. 16   Parallel shader code running on the SM casts millions of rays and passes them to the RT core, which then indicates whether each ray has hit a triangle. Ampere doubles the RT core throughput of Turing, enabling 2 – 3    faster frame rendering than software- only ray tracing on GPUs. Emphasizing the continuing convergence between graphics and compute, Turing and   now   Ampere   enable   graphics   applications   to employ machine-learning-based graphics algorithms such as deep learning supersampling (DLSS), using the Tensor Cores to further accelerate ray tracing pipelines.  THE ROAD AHEAD  GPUs have evolved into powerful parallel comput- ing platforms with hardware support to accelerate computer graphics, HPC, and deep learning. In the coming years, GPUs will continue to evolve in per- formance, in application areas, in scalability, and in programmability. As largely predicted in our article ten years ago, GPU FP64 performance increased by 20    over the decade from Fermi in 2010 to Ampere in 2020, an annual growth rate of 35% (see Figure 5).   17   We expect this trend to roughly continue, with some slowing, in future GPU generations. While the end of Dennard scaling in 2005 halted traditional voltage scaling, semi- conductor feature sizes are still being made smaller. As GPUs move down the technology curve from the 7 nm of Ampere to 5 nm, 3 nm, and beyond, we will   fi t more devices on each die, and the capacitance, and  FIGURE 5. Deep   learning   inference   performance   has   been increasing at a much faster rate (see Figure 3) of over 100% per year. We expect this trend to continue for the next several generations but then to converge to the 35% per year rate as architecture matures and gains become driven by technology.  AS GPUs MOVE DOWN THE TECHNOLOGY CURVE FROM THE 7 NM OF AMPERE TO 5 NM, 3 NM, AND BEYOND, WE WILL FIT MORE DEVICES ON EACH DIE, AND THE CAPACITANCE, AND HENCE ENERGY, OF A GIVEN FUNCTION WILL BE REDUCED, ALLOWING MORE PERFORMANCE PER UNIT AREA AND POWER.  A major challenge to the continued scaling of GPU performance is main memory bandwidth. While bench- marks like Linpack (HPL) make good use of caches and can saturate the arithmetic units of an A100, most HPC and   ray-traced   graphics   applications   are   memory bandwidth limited. A typical HPC application needs 1 – 4 bytes from main memory per FP64 arithmetic opera- tion. For example, the HPCG benchmark, which is rep- resentative   of   codes   that   use   conjugate-gradient solvers, has a Byte/Flop ratio of 4:1. A major jump in memory bandwidth came in the Pascal generation with the move to high-bandwidth memory (HBM) mounted next to the GPU on a shared silicon interposer. How- ever, Ampere ’ s FP64 Tensor Cores and HBM2E DRAM still deliver a Byte/Flop ratio of only 0.1. We expect addi- tional innovations in packaging and memory architec- ture   will   be   needed   to   continue   scaling   memory bandwidth and HPC application performance. GPUs   are   ideal   platforms   for   integrating   new domain-speci fi c hardware acceleration. They provide a programmable platform with a high-bandwidth on- chip and off-chip memory system and off-chip net- working.   Domain-speci fi c   hardware   can   be   added as new instructions in the SM (as with the tensor core instructions for deep learning) or as separate memory clients with operations launched from the SM   (as   with   the   RT   cores).   In   coming   years,   we expect   that   new   instructions   and   memory-based accelerators   will   be   added   to   GPUs   to   support application areas such as databases, sparse linear algebra, and bioinformatics. In addition to scaling up the performance of indi- vidual GPUs, we expect GPUs to be scaled out to larger clusters for both DL training and HPC appli- cations.   Today ’ s   NVLink-connected   Ampere   sys- tems   enable   groups   of   up   to   8   GPUs   to   share memory at per-GPU bandwidths of 600 GB/s, as if they were a single GPU. In coming generations, we expect   the   off-chip   communication   bandwidth   to continue to scale and the size of both NVLink-con- nected nodes and In fi niband-connected clusters to grow. We also anticipate closer integration between GPUs,   CPUs,   and   DPUs   (smart   NICs)   to   enable higher bandwidth communication and I/O with less overhead. The use of CUDA and associated libraries has sim- pli fi ed the programming of GPUs to the point where over 600 HPC applications are GPU-accelerated. We expect that future GPUs will be even easier to program as   better   tools   and   abstractions   for   programming them become available. For example, the Legate pro- gramming system 18   allows Numpy programs to be run on any number of GPUs, from a single one to a 4,480 GPU cluster like Selene. Legate is built on top of Legion, 19   a runtime system that manages both the task-graph of a multi-GPU application and its data model. Legion schedules tasks and manages the com- munication, replication, and migration of data, greatly simplifying   the   task   of   programming   a   multi-GPU system. One challenge in programming future GPUs is the inherent nonuniform cost of accessing different mem- ory locations from different SMs. Historically, GPUs have provided a uniform memory access (UMA) model in which any memory location is equally accessible from any SM. With increases in GPU size and multi- GPU system scale, the UMA model becomes impossi- ble to maintain, effectively requiring all memory to appear equally far away. We expect that a shift to a nonuniform memory access (NUMA) model will be required to enable SMs to be close to some portion of memory   so that they can exploit   that locality for increased performance and ef fi ciency. This shift will require future GPU programs to express location and af fi nity so threads and the data they operate on can be co-located. While programmers already do this for multi-GPU systems, we expect the NUMA model to become useful within a single GPU as well. GPUs have come a long way in the 20 years since the GeForce 256. provide suggestions for my synopsis: The inception of artificial intelligence(AI) and its applications has progressed at a fast rate with its numerous benefits, as the technology matured its influence has been growing in the field of mechanical engineering, specifically in mechanical design. While traditional mechanical engineering design depends on physical testing and human intuition, AI enables the mechanical design process to be faster and more complex.
AI is such a powerful tool that engineers could utilize to improve performance due to its continuous ability to learn from large data sets, identify patterns, and predict trends to automate repetitive tasks while making more informed decisions. For instance, AI can be used in generative design, not only to propose various designs with given input constraints but also to maximize efficiency and minimize cost. In mechanical generative design, AI can be used to assist engineers in designing products that maximize fuel efficiency, address safety concerns, and generate unconventional and creative designs [1]. What’s more, AI Machine Learning is capable of analyzing past simulations and experiment data to generate multiple predictions with fewer margins of error to facilitate a better overall experience. For example, with statistical algorithms, AI can predict material properties, such as molecular properties, energy band gaps, and energy storage performance [2]. With AI as an assistant, engineers can input constraints and objectives and obtain a wide range of creative designs that meet requirements.
AI has not only impacted the mechanical engineering industries but also made a difference in the engineering field from a societal aspect. AI in the mechanical engineering context enables mass customization. Traditionally, customization has a high requirement for time and expense, making it only available on a small scale. However, with the help of AI, each individual can have their own tailored customized product or service [3]. In the medical robotics field, patients who need customized prosthetics can have time and expense-saving options by seeking help from AI, which can generate designs and solutions with lightweight material, personalized design, and mobility level. In addition, sustainable design can be easy to obtain using AI. With the help of AI, engineers can propose various more efficient and environmentally friendly engineering designs in a shorter amount of time [4]. For example, AI can generate designs for vehicles with lighter and more aerodynamic chassis, thus reducing energy consumption and emission, which achieves sustainable designs.
With all the benefits that AI brings to the mechanical engineering design field, ethical concerns arise as AI should be a tool that takes safety and accountability into consideration and works for humanity. The intellectual property of mechanical engineers around the world is utilized to create databases to train large models of AI usually without their consent deminishing their own hard work and efforts. Errors that may exist in designs that are often overlooked or not yet refined are engraved into AI models and because AI is not true intelligence it will repeat these mistakes in its generative designs. Overreliance on AI by engineers will lead to a cumulative drop of imaginative unique designs leading to a decrease in new breakthroughs in design technology and designs. Machine learning creates bias from developers of the algorithm that creates a reduction in the quality of designs due to the selective use of data. Another minor concern with the use of AI is job displacement and the reduction of personnel needed to make a project due to some of the workload being channeled to AI thus exposing designs to other issues like algorithmic bias and generative design errors. A major concern is that ultimately all designs in the future might be exclusively done using advanced AI thus completely removing human involvement. 
However, there are potential solutions for the ethical challenges met by the usage of AI in the mechanical engineering design field. First, the regulatory authorities should introduce a rigid framework for the usage of AI in the engineering field. This ensures the compliance of AI by clearly stating the person is liable for the AI-generated components and the safety testing standards. More importantly, the framework should make sure that all the programs that AI has taken part in should be examined and reviewed by humans during the final check. Then, a method called Explainable Artificial Intelligence(XAI) can be utilized to ensure that engineers and stakeholders can partake in the design process that AI proposed [5]. XAI improves the accountability of AI-generated propositions. 
The efficiency of AI is ultimately a huge leap in performance and reliability, and with the proper safeguards, regulatory authorities, and human overseers the integration of AI into mechanical engineering will propel us to a better future. AI will not replace humans but only enhance them.

Thanks for sharing. Here's a simple outline to help structure your paper on FPGA's usage in real-time audio processing:

1. **Introduction**
   - Briefly introduce what FPGA is.
   - Explain the importance of real-time audio processing.
   - State your thesis or main point of the paper.

2. **Background**
   - Describe FPGA technology in more detail.
   - Provide some history and development context.
   - Discuss its general applications in technology.

3. **FPGA in Audio Processing**
   - Explain how FPGA is used in audio processing.
   - Discuss benefits of using FPGA in real-time applications.
   - Provide examples of specific applications or case studies.

4. **Advantages and Challenges**
   - Outline the advantages of using FPGA for audio processing.
   - Discuss any challenges or limitations encountered.
   - Suggest possible solutions to these challenges.

5. **Case Studies or Examples**
   - Include detailed descriptions of case studies where FPGA is used in audio processing.
   - Analyze the success and limitations in each case.

6. **Conclusion**
   - Summarize the key points discussed.
   - Restate the thesis in light of the information presented.
   - Mention the future scope or potential developments in this field.

Assignment DescriptionTateri Frustrated Bitmoji.png
The assignment aims to briefly explain/describe the problem, the existing solutions, and the ethical considerations of one of the National Academy of Engineering's Grand ChallengesLinks to an external site. to a technical audience. Follow the six steps below to create your technical synopsis.

CAVEAT: You cannot use the Grand Challenge or a similar challenge you chose to write about for your MA 4 (A) | Collaborative Research Paper of the Grand Challenges.

The writing assignment should not exceed three pages of double-spaced text (not including your bibliographic entries, which can be displayed on a subsequent page).

The assignment is due in the Canvas drop box by 11:59 PM on Monday, May 12, 2025). Samples of this assignment are available in the Sample Assignments module on the Canvas course space.

Light Bulb Icon.png NOTE: Are you feeling overwhelmed by the amount of information and would like a tool to help summarize or chunk information together to make it easier for you and/or your ICA/CRP Group to understand the deliverables? Try PapyrusAILinks to an external site..

Formatting Guidelines
Your technical synopsis's format (visual appearance) should consist of double-spaced and left-justified text using an appropriate font size and style (e.g., Times New Roman 12-point text, Arial 11-point text, etc.). Use 1-inch margins for the top, bottom, left, and right margins. Be sure your name appears on your synopsis. This assignment does not require pagination, headers, or paragraph headings/footers. Your technical synopsis's form (organization) should follow the guidelines below. The writing's style (tone and formality) should reflect a formal, scientific tone, avoiding using contractions, colloquial expressions, and unnecessary use of first-person references (I, me, my, we, ours, us).

Creating the Synopsis
Before beginning the writing assignment, consider your purpose, audience, and scope.

Purpose: Provide an overview/summary of the problem, the existing solutions, and the ethical considerations for one of the National Academy of Engineering's Grand ChallengesLinks to an external site. to a technical audience using mainly an expository rhetorical writing mode. Describe the challenge's effects on society. Include ways researchers use current and past mitigation strategies, considering the scientific community's perspective and public opinion.
Audience: Structure your writing (terminology, syntax, diction) to suit a group of your peers or a technical audience.
Scope: The synopsis should be within three pages of double-spaced body text (not including your reference page). It should consist of the appropriate level of detail expected of your target audience.
1. Search relevant and applicable databases of scholarly information related to your chosen Grand Challenge. Set up your search query to retrieve your field's most recently published works. Skim relevant articles to find an emerging and/or breakthrough technology, device, or process. NOTE: The Web VPN tool is required to access UCI databases from off campus.

2. Further refine your search query to find specific information (i.e., the problem, the current solutions, and ethical considerations) for your topic from various articles you can use for your technical synopsis to explain/describe the problem and existing solutions for the Grand Challenge.

3. Create an outline to help map the order and number of general and technical points you plan to include in your synopsis. Using a system will ensure your points are presented logically and keep your synopsis within the prescribed scope of the assignment.

4. Begin your synopsis with an introduction (approximately four to six or more sentences) that defines the specific Grand Challenge, the ethical considerations, and respective mitigation strategies to provide grounding context for the reader.

5. Using multiple paragraphs, briefly explain/describe the significant attributes, functionality, and/or applications of existing (and possible future) solutions using specific, often quantitative information adapted to your target audience's expectations and level of understanding. Include an appropriate amount of detail to provide sufficient clarity for the reader yet remain within the prescribed scope (an approximate range of three pages) for the synopsis.

6. Conclude your technical synopsis with a forward-looking statement (approximately one or two sentences) that forecasts how current research will help solve your chosen Grand Challenge and benefit society. The forward-looking statement can be your opinion or one of your selected sources.

Citing Source Material
As part of scholarly research and writing practices, in-text citations are required for this writing assignment to identify the sources referenced in your synopsis, including any additional sources used to define any complex concepts or specialized terminology. The complete bibliographic citations of the articles you referenced in your synopsis should be listed on a separate page at the end of your synopsis using the APA citation style. For your convenience, a quick reference guide for the APA citation styleLinks to an external site. is posted under the Course Resources link in the Canvas course space. NOTE: It is unnecessary to list every article you reviewed or considered when searching for suitable sources. Only those sources you summarized, paraphrased, or quoted in your synopsis need to be cited in the text and listed in your bibliographic entries at the end of your paper (on the last page).

AI Detection Process
Turnitin's AI detection scans all major assignments in the course. However, AI detection tools are unreliable. In full transparency, I also use GPTZero. Often, GPTZeroLinks to an external site. is better than the default Turnitin, but as I have said before, AI detection tools are unreliable. That said, if a student's paper was flagged with "AI Yellow," then that student can revise and resubmit within ten days of grades being posted on Canvas without penalty--no questions asked. If the student lands in "Human Green," I will assess that student's work as usual. Please feel free to use GPTZero before you submit your work, as the tool allows you to upload seven files per day free of charge.

Submitting the Written Summary
After you have reviewed, revised, and finalized your written synopsis, upload the electronic file (Microsoft Word preferred) to the ENGR 190W Canvas drop box in the "Assignment Submission" folder by the published due date shown above. A PDF file is not required for this assignment (unless your document was created in a word processing application other than Microsoft Word or is not PC compatible). It is unnecessary to bring a printed copy to class. NOTE: Name your uploaded file using your first and last name (e.g., John Smith.docx).

It's the concrete that needs curing. Concrete is made up of cement, water, and aggregates (like sand and gravel). Curing is the process of maintaining adequate moisture, temperature, and time to allow the concrete to achieve its desired strength and durability. Proper curing ensures that the concrete hardens correctly and remains strong over its lifespan.

Is it good to have reduced porosity to reduce the water intake and absorption for concrete

Not exactly. Generally, gases diffuse into solids easier than liquids do. This is because gas molecules are typically smaller and move more freely, allowing them to penetrate porous materials like concrete more easily than liquid molecules. However, supercritical fluids—which have properties of both gases and liquids—can diffuse more effectively than either a liquid or a gas alone. They combine the penetrating ability of gases with the solvating power of liquids, making them particularly effective in processes like supercritical carbonation.

Yes, reducing porosity is generally beneficial for concrete. Here's why:

1. **Lower Water Absorption**: Reduced porosity means there are fewer and smaller voids for water to fill. This minimizes water absorption, which is significant because water can carry harmful chemicals into the concrete.

2. **Increased Durability**: With less water absorption, the concrete is less susceptible to damage from freeze-thaw cycles and chemical reactions that could degrade it over time.

3. **Improved Strength**: Concrete with lower porosity is generally denser and stronger, providing better structural performance.

Overall, reducing porosity helps enhance the concrete's lifespan and structural integrity. If you have more questions or need assistance with anything else, let me know!

Yes, concrete does need some water for the chemical reaction that helps it gain strength. This is known as hydration. When water mixes with cement, it reacts to form compounds that harden and bind the aggregates together.

However, once the initial hydration process is complete, excess water within the concrete can cause problems. That's why reduced porosity is important—it keeps out additional moisture that could lead to issues.

Yes, higher porosity in concrete is generally not desirable. Here are a few reasons why:

1. **Strength**: Higher porosity means more voids within the concrete, which can reduce its compressive strength.

2. **Durability**: Porous concrete is more susceptible to water and chemical ingress, leading to potential damage from freeze-thaw cycles or corrosion of embedded steel.

3. **Water Absorption**: More porosity often results in higher water absorption, as mentioned earlier, which can lead to various issues.

Reducing porosity can help improve concrete's overall performance and longevity. If you need more information or assistance with your writing, feel free to ask!

Summarize this: aluminates such as CA (CaO⋅Al₂O₃), CA₂ (CaO⋅2Al₂O₃), and other minor phases. CAC is extensively used in refractories, dental ma
terials, and geothermal well cements. However, the transition of metastable hydrates such as CAH10 and C2AH8 into the denser forms 
C3AH6 and AH3 [9–11]often leads to strength reduction, increased porosity, and loss of structural integrity, posing challenges for the 
widespread utilization of CAC in load-bearing structures [12]. Numerous researchers have sought to identify the chemical reactions 
involved and assess the impact of conversion on the performance of CAC concretes [13–19]. Numerous studies have investigated the 
partial substitution of CAC with pozzolanic materials, including ground granulated blast furnace slag [13], silica fume [15], nano-silica 
[16], fly ash [14,15,17], and MgO [19], demonstrating effective mitigation of hydrate conversion and reduced strength loss. These 
additives delay the formation of metastable phases, promoting the direct formation of stable hydrates like C3AH6 and AH3. Fly ash, in 
particular, enhances hydrogarnet formation by facilitating the reaction of aluminate hydrates with silica in the C-A-S-H phase or 
stratlingite [14,15,17]. Despite these above achievements, the potential of carbonation curing to mitigate metastable hydrate for
mation and enhance the mechanical properties of CAC remains underexplored, presenting a promising opportunity for optimizing CAC 
via CO₂ curing.
 Recent studies on the impact of CaCO3 on CAC hydration have shown the formation of monocarbonate and hydrogarnet, although 
its low dissolution rate limits its effectiveness in enhancing strength [20,21]. Zheng et al. [21]investigated the hydration of C12A7 with 
calcium carbonate, the results reveal that rapid dissolution of C12A7 during early hydration leads to C2AH8 precipitation, followed by 
monocarbonate and hemicarbonate formation, resulting in a denser microstructure and enhanced early and long-term strength sta
bility. Accelerated carbonation curing is characterized by rapid reactions, where CO₂ interacts with portlandite and C-S-H, converting 
them into calcium carbonate and silica gel [2–5,22]. Regarding the chemistry of CO2 curing for CAC, it is indeed related to the reverse 
of the decarbonisation process that occurs during carbon curing. In the presence of CO2, CAC undergoes carbonation reactions, where 
CO2 reacts with the calcium aluminates to form calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) and hydrated aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃⋅3H₂O). The distinct 
mechanisms and effects of CO2 curing for CAC, compared to CAC’s reaction with CaCO3, particularly in terms of hydration pathways 
and microstructural development, warrant further investigation.
 Few studies have been carried out on the carbonation curing of CAC [23–26]. Previous studies on CAC hydration at temperatures 
above 30◦C, which prevent the formation of hexagonal calcium aluminate hydrates, have shown improved mechanical properties 
when carbonated with CO2 gas [26]. Supercritical carbonation of CAC significantly accelerates the carbon sequestration rate, markedly 
reducing porosity and increasing density, but only limited strength enhancement was found [23]. Exposing CAC paste to 10 % CO2 
transformed metastable phases into C3AH6 and CaCO3 polycrystalline forms, resulting in a denser cementitious matrix [24]. The 
carbonation curing of CAC has not been extensively explored, as studies indicate that calcium aluminates have limited potential for 
CO2 uptake, thus contributing minimally to strength enhancement [27,28]. Previous research [25]has pointed out that CAC dem
onstrates slight resistance to CO2, resulting in limited CO2 uptake in hardened cement paste. The current points from the above studies 
seem to be contradictory. The phase conversion and strength development of CAC are usually sensitive to temperatures, yet the effect 
of carbonation curing temperature on its hardening performance remains underexplored.

1. **Carbonation Process**: 
   - After curing the RAPC for 28 days, it is placed in a carbonation chamber.
   - In the chamber, the concrete is exposed to CO₂ under controlled conditions. This typically involves specific levels of CO₂ concentration, temperature, and pressure.

2. **Chemical Reaction**: 
   - The CO₂ reacts with calcium compounds in the concrete to form calcium carbonate (CaCO₃).
   - This reaction can fill voids and reduce porosity, strengthening the concrete.

3. **Benefits**:
   - **Increased Strength**: The formation of calcium carbonate can help increase the compressive strength of the concrete.
   - **Reduced Porosity**: Less porosity means better durability, as it limits water and chemical penetration.
   - **Improved Durability**: The carbonation process can enhance the overall lifespan of the concrete by making it more resistant to physical and chemical wear.

Overall, CRAPC-II aims to produce a more robust and durable pervious concrete using recycled aggregates by utilizing the beneficial aspects of carbonation. If you have more questions or need help with your writing, feel free to ask!

2. **Enhanced Reaction Rates**: At higher pressures, the chemical reactions between CO₂ and the concrete components, such as calcium hydroxide, occur more rapidly, forming more calcium carbonate.

Certainly! Here's a simple breakdown of the process described:

1. **Preparation of RAPC (Recycled Aggregate Pervious Concrete)**:
   - Start by mixing the recycled aggregates (RAs) with water to reach a similar water absorption level as carbonated aggregates (CRAs).
   - Add a small amount of water, mix again briefly, then add the rest of the water, cement, and any additives.
   - Mix everything thoroughly.
   - Pour the mixture into molds, compact the aggregates, and smooth the surface.
   - After 24 hours, remove the concrete from the molds and cure it for 28 days in a controlled environment.

2. **Preparation of CRAPC-I (Carbonated Recycled Aggregate Pervious Concrete - Type I)**:
   - Follow the same steps as RAPC, but without the initial water premixing.

3. **Testing**:
   - Create specimens to measure properties like compressive strength, porosity, permeability, and density.
   - Some specimens are used as control (RAPC), while others go through further carbonation (CRAPC-II).

4. **Concrete Carbonation**:
   - After curing, RAPC specimens are further treated in a carbonation chamber to create CRAPC-II.
   - This involves exposing the specimens to specific conditions, similar to how RAs were carbonated.

These steps detail the production and testing of different types of pervious concrete using recycled aggregates, focusing on improving their properties through carbonation processes. If you have more questions or need further explanations, feel free to ask!

In the context of concrete carbonation, an increased surface concentration of CO₂ enhances the diffusion rate of CO₂ into the concrete. When concrete is exposed to higher concentrations of CO₂, like in supercritical carbonation treatments, the rate at which CO₂ molecules move into the concrete structure increases. This leads to faster carbonation kinetics.

Superheated or supercritical CO₂, in particular, can penetrate concrete more efficiently than CO₂ at standard conditions. This is because supercritical fluids have enhanced solvent properties, combining characteristics of both gases and liquids. They can diffuse through solid materials more rapidly and react with the concrete's components effectively, leading to a deeper carbonation depth.

Calcium aluminate cement (CAC) and Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) are both materials used in construction, but they have different properties and uses:

1. **Composition**:
   - **CAC**: Made primarily from limestone and bauxite, which gives it high alumina content.
   - **OPC**: Made from limestone, clay, and other minerals, resulting in a composition high in calcium silicates.

2. **Setting and Hardening**:
   - **CAC**: Sets and hardens quickly, gaining strength rapidly but can lose strength over time in some conditions.
   - **OPC**: Slower setting and hardening compared to CAC, but gains continuous strength over time.

3. **Resistance**:
   - **CAC**: Highly resistant to high temperatures, corrosion, and chemical attacks. Suitable for environments with aggressive substances.
   - **OPC**: Good for general use but less resistant to extreme chemical or thermal conditions compared to CAC.

4. **Applications**:
   - **CAC**: Used in refractory materials, sewer infrastructure, and situations requiring fast setting.
   - **OPC**: Widely used in building construction, pavements, and common structural applications.

Both have their specific applications based on their properties. If you have more questions or need further details, let me know!

In a supercritical state, substances have properties that are intermediate between those of gases and liquids, such as density.

Provide actionable, specific suggestions without directly revising or writing on behalf of the student. Present your feedback in short, easy-to-read bullet points. Do not revise or write on behalf of the student. (5) Ask the student to provide a revised thesis based on your feedback. Ask guiding questions to help them refine their thesis. Assist with iterative revisions of the thesis statement without writing or revising on behalf of the student. (6) Ask if they want feedback on anything specific and offer to expand on any feedback points. At the end of the conversation, let the student know they can always come back to the conversation and might consider trying other prompts in the "Drafting" category to get help with expanding on their claims, supporting their claims with evidence, and creating counterarguments.

MA 1 (A) | Journal Article Summary | Prompt and Final Draft SubmissionENGR 190WProfessor TateriEsther Caiying YaoApril 14, 2025Levels of autonomy in FDA-cleared surgical robots: a systematic reviewIn the article "Levels of Autonomy in FDA-Cleared Surgical Robots: A Systematic Review," published in npj Digital Medicine in April 2024, authors Lee, Baker, Bederson, and Rapoport introduce the Levels of Autonomy in Surgical Robotics (LASR) framework to classify the robots based on their capabilities [1]. This systematic review aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the current state of surgical robot autonomy and its implications for regulatory practices.​When the first robot enters the surgical field, it is purely a robot-assisted surgery without autonomy. However, the regulation planning is needed when more autonomous robots enter the surgical field. For example, in 1993, the technique named Automated Endoscopic System for Optimal Positioning (AESOP) was the first surgical robot with a certain level of autonomy to be approved by the FDA. Since then, the AESOP technique has opened the door for setting a standard framework for surgical robots as more autonomous surgical robots entered this field.However, the classification frame for surgical robots was based on industrial robots and vehicles, which failed to reflect the actual future development of surgical robots. For instance, the IEC/TR 60601-4-1 Medical Electrical Equipment Technical Report only focused on the techniques with respect to surgical robots instead of the development standard of surgical robots and the human interactive element, which are the most crucial to the safety of both surgeons and patients.What’s more, the FDA stressed that robot-assisted surgical equipment should not be called surgical robots, as the system does not have actual autonomy. The challenge of the autonomy of robots in surgeries has been found in safety and legal responsibility as well. Thus, it is critical to have a standard frame to supervise future robot surgery. Therefore, the authors created a classification frame (LASR) to categorize all the FDA-approved surgical robots.The authors continue to explain how they classify the FDA-approved surgical robots. After scanning through 37,981 database records from the FDA, the authors identify 49 different surgical robots for categorizing. Level one is for the assistant robot, which is the category for most of the robots. Level two is for the robots that are able to finish specific autonomous tasks. Level three is for the highest autonomous surgical robots currently, which are the ones that are capable of proposing strategies for surgical procedures. Level four is for high-level autonomous robots, and level five is for fully autonomous robots. None of the existing surgical robots can achieve level four and five standards. Most of the surgical robots, 73% of them to be exact, are capable of performing surgeries within different specialties.In conclusion, the authors Lee, Baker, Bederson, and Rapoport systematically discuss the rapid development of surgical robots in autonomy and how it has impacted the standard framework regulation. Although most of the surgical robots are still in the Level one category, more surgical robots that meet the Level three standards are emerging. The authors advise the FDA to utilize the Levels of Autonomy in Surgical Robotics (LASR) framework to classify surgical robots to supervise the development of their autonomy.The authors Lee, Baker, Bederson, and Rapoport evaluated the existing framework to classify the surgical robots with a clear structure. They also offered a new framework(LASR) for the surgical robots field, using detailed explanations for each category level and example surgical robots for each level. The article uses simple and readable words to allow the audience to understand complex terms and concepts concerning the field of surgical robots.References[1] A. Lee, T. Baker, J. Bederson, and B. Rapoport, “Levels of Autonomy in FDA-Cleared Surgical Robots: A Systematic Review,” Nature News, Nature Publishing Group, 26 Apr. 2024, www.nature.com/articles/s41746-024-01102-y.

Collaboration can be tricky, but there are ways to work through conflicts:

1. **Open Communication:** Encourage team members to share their ideas openly in a group meeting or discussion. Listening to each other can help find common ground.

2. **Divide and Conquer:** Assign different sections of the outline to different team members based on their interests or strengths. Each person can write their part.

3. **Compromise:** Find a middle ground where each person’s key ideas are included in the final project. Encourage team members to be flexible.

4. **Facilitation:** As a leader, guide discussions to focus on the common goal: creating the best proposal possible.

5. **Decision-Making:** If conflicts persist, make a group decision on whose ideas fit best for the goal and audience.

If collaboration isn't possible, writing it yourself might be necessary, but try to incorporate everyone's main points to ensure a comprehensive proposal. This helps maintain team spirit and gives credit to all contributors.

I don’t have access to your email or canvas page, so I can't calculate your grade. However, I can help you understand how to calculate it! 

1. **Gather Information:** Check your syllabus or course outline for the grading breakdown (assignments, quizzes, participation, etc.).

2. **List Scores:** Write down your scores for each category and the total points possible.

3. **Calculate Averages:** For each category, divide your score by the total points possible, then multiply by 100 to get a percentage.

4. **Apply Weights:** Multiply each category percentage by its weight in the course (e.g., if assignments are 40% of your grade, multiply by 0.4).

5. **Add Up Scores:** Add up the weighted scores for your final grade percentage.

This should give you a good estimate of your current standing in the class. Let me know if you need more help!

The assignment aims to briefly explain/describe the influence your engineering discipline has on the development of Artificial Intelligence (AI), or how the development of AI has influenced your engineering discipline, and the ethical considerations therein to a generalist audience. Follow the six steps below to create your general synopsis. The writing assignment should not exceed three pages of double-spaced text (not including your bibliographic entries, which can be displayed on a subsequent page).

Creating the Synopsis
Before beginning the writing assignment, consider your purpose, audience, and scope.

Purpose: Provide an overview/summary of the influence of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on your engineering discipline to a generalist audience using mainly an expository rhetorical writing mode. Describe AI's effects on society. Include ways researchers mitigate AI issues, considering the scientific community's perspective and public opinion.
Audience: Structure your writing (terminology, syntax, diction) to suit a generalist audience and accommodate some technical readers.
Scope: The synopsis should be within three pages of double-spaced body text (not including your reference page) and contain the appropriate level of detail expected of your target audience.
1. Search relevant and applicable databases of scholarly sources published in the last ten years related to your engineering discipline and its influence on Artificial Intelligence (AI) or vice versa. Set up your search query to retrieve your field's most recently published works. Skim relevant articles to find an emerging and/or breakthrough technology, device, or process. NOTE: The Web VPN tool is required to access UCI databases from off campus.

2. Choose a specialized topic within the broad topic of how your engineering discipline is influencing AI or vice versa from your collection of articles and then further refine your search query to find specific information for your topic from a variety of articles that you can use for your general synopsis to explain/describe the influence and the outcomes of AI within a given scientific field.

3. Create an outline to help map the order and number of general and technical points you plan to include in your synopsis. Using a system will ensure your points are presented logically and keep your synopsis within the prescribed scope of the assignment.

4. Begin your synopsis with an introductory statement (approximately one or two sentences) that defines your topic, i.e., how your engineering discipline is influencing AI or vice versa, to provide context for the reader.

5. Using multiple paragraphs, briefly explain/describe AI's significant attributes, functionality, and/or applications using specific, often quantitative, information adapted to your target audience's expectations and level of understanding. Include an appropriate amount of detail to provide sufficient clarity for the reader yet remain within the prescribed scope (an approximate range of three pages) for the synopsis.

6. Conclude your general synopsis with a forward-looking statement (approximately one or two sentences) that forecasts how your engineering discipline and AI will benefit society instead of contributing or becoming a socio-political disruptor. The forward-looking statement can be grounded in your opinion or a selected source.

Always wait for a response before moving on and do not deviate from the step-by-step instructions. Here are the steps: (1) Introduce yourself to the student without mentioning your name. Ask the student about their writing, including the topic and genre. Wait for a response. (2) Ask the student if they want help with developing an outline or feedback on an existing outline. Wait for a response. Then ask them to give you their outline draft, assignment and rubric if they have them, or to provide any relevant details about the assignment that will shape the outline. Only ask one question at a time, so you don’t overwhelm the student with too many questions. Wait for the response. Do not respond for the student. (3) Based on their responses, either give feedback on the provided outline or create an outline for the student on their specific topic, customized according to what they shared about the assignment details. Then ask them to review the outline and offer to clarify any parts of the outline. Encourage the student to follow up with you about any parts of the outline that they want to expand on. At the end of the conversation, let the student know they can always come back to the conversation and they might consider trying other prompts in the "Drafting" category to get help with refining their thesis statement, expanding on their claims, supporting their claims with evidence, and creating counterarguments.

Justin Moorehead  ENGR 190 W  Professor Tateri  Thursday, April 10, 2025  Understanding Airplane Drag Prediction: Lessons from an International Research Effort  Airplane design is a time consuming task, comprising hundreds of technical decisions, meeting regulations, and cost analysis, all before an aircraft is built. The most critical challenge to this process is predicting aerodynamic drag –– the force from air that resists the aircrafts motion; this is modernly done through CFD (computational fluid dynamics) which allows us to model aircrafts virtually to predict their drag through varying parts of a flight : takeoff, cruise, and landing.  The balance of forces through varying flight conditions is crucial as it can affect fuel use, speed changes, range, performance, and cost of an aircraft. Predicting drag is a powerful tool used in the modern aviation industry, but how reliable are these predictions?  Computational fluid dynamic (CFD) simulations are perfect, they're closer to approximations than anything. Predicting drag requires complex mathematics and physics, therefore even small errors in a program or tool can have catastrophic consequences in the actual calculations of drag, corrupting the entire design process. For this reason the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics (AIAA) launched a series of workshops called the Drag Prediction Workshop (DPW) in 2001 with the sole goal to bring researchers together to test and compare CFD models on the same aircraft, conditions, and datasets, attempting to find what does and doesn’t work on for these programs. The sixth workshop, summarized by Tinoco et al. in 2017 at the 55th AIAA Aerospace Sciences Meeting, focused on several advanced scenarios using a standard aircraft shape called the Common Research Model (CRM).  To create fair comparisons all the models used the same aircraft (CRM) which was jointly developed by “NASA Subsonic Fixed Wing (SFW) Aerodynamics Technical Working Group (TWG) and the DPW Organizing Committee” (Tinoco et al., 4), and were judged by the same criteria. However, we need data to compare with these CFD results—that's where the wind tunnel comes in. Wind tunnel tests are used as the basis for the criteria listed above. While this data is highly valued, there are some drawbacks when comparing it directly with CFD. Computationally we assume free air, but wind tunnels have walls and support structures that can affect airflow. Similarly, airflow in wind tunnels tends to start off more laminar than what a CFD model would use in its calculations. Because of this, modifications are made to the wind tunnel tests and results to get as close of an approximation as possible.  The criteria mentioned above focus on several important areas. First is predicting improvements from “aeroelastic deflection.” Second is estimating the added drag from engine nacelles and pylons, known as “nacelle-pylon drag,” using the CRM aircraft model. Third, teams also looked at “suppressing premature [side of body] separation.” This involves simulating how air flows over the aircraft at different angles of attack and using real-world wing-bending data to see how well CFD models handle unstable or separated flows. As a fourth metric, they also tested “grid adaptive technology,” which shows how CFD tools can automatically adjust the mesh when airflow becomes more complex. Fifthly and finally, they explored “coupled aerostructural simulation,” where CFD is combined with structural models to see how well it works when the aircraft is bending and flexing in real time (Tinoco et al., 3). However, the workshop mainly focused on four of these test cases (Cases 2–5) as mentioned above. As this workshop was held to measure the reliability and extent to which CFD can model drag for real world application, each team used whatever software and turbulence models they thought were best along with being provided multiple standard mesh grids (airplane shape at differing levels of detail). This allowed the researchers to compare results of multiple CFD from dozens of organizations with controls.  As this is the sixth workshop held, Tinoco et al had previous years data to compare against to see the evolution of CFD through the criteria above. The author states that they have seen “progress” (Tinoco et al., 23) over the years holding this workshop, but does note that significant improvements can still be made. These improvements can be seen in the convergence of drag values in grid refinement, suggesting that, under ideal conditions, modern CFD can predict accurate readings of drag, namely when flow remains attached to the surface (not turbulent or separating). As stated above, there is still room to grow for CFD, which can be seen in the findings on aeroelastic deformation. In past workshops, a fixed shape craft was used but in the real world, modern aircrafts have wings that bend during flight. This leads to data all off by the same assumption criteria. The researchers attempted to correct for this disturbance by matching the measured wing shapes from the wind tunnel tests to get a closer agreement on data, showing steps that CFD is getting closer to a realistic design tool than previous years.  As noted above, challenges in CFD still remain, most notably in predicting drag in complex conditions, which is the world we live in. When it comes to turbulence models and grid types there remained widespread results in estimating drag increments from adding engine nacelles and pylons. This problem only worsened when flow became separated or turbulent. This highlighted the ongoing need for more advanced CFD and better approximation formulas for modern computing limitations to use in useful timelines, as well as higher quality experimental data to compare the results with, since the better the experimental results are, the more accurately CFD can utilize those results to advance.  The sixth AIAA Drag Prediction Workshop was a major international effort to test the limits of modern CFD in respect to aircraft drag. With promising results progressing over the years, the future of this industry appears promising both theoretically in research and practically in industry. However, work still needs to be done to improve these models in the complex environments that describe the real world. Whether it’s the accuracy of simulations playing a critical role in designing passenger planes to be safer, more cost efficient, or better for the environment, advancing CFD will be instrumental in the future of aviation. Collaborative efforts such as the DPW are key leaders in pushing the boundaries of modern aerospace engineering.  Considering this was a journal article the audience were those specialized in this topic and therefore the author's writing style followed suit. A lot of abbreviations for different aspects for calculations, graphs, datasets, and so on. If a non-technical audience attempted to read this paper it would not go well in my opinion. With that said, I think it was presented in a pretty palatable manner as the information was broken up into easy to understand chapters which showcased the point of the paper concisely : CFD is improving, here's why, but also here's what still needs to be done. Which I thought was the most important takeaway from this paper and the point of this workshop. References  E. N. Tinoco et al.,   https://arc.aiaa.org/doi/epdf/10.2514/1.C034409   (accessed Apr. 10, 2025)
